In contrast to kidney involvement, genotype does not appear to be a significant predictor of the severity of polycystic liver disease in patients with ADPKD [73]. Pharmacologic agents to prevent ADPKD progression Pathophysiologic mechanisms of cyst enlargement in ADPKD involve a number of biological pathways. The cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) pathway is upregulated in PKD cystic epithelia [74], leading to increased cell proliferation and fluid secretion [10]. cAMP activity can be downregulated by antagonism of arginine vasopressin (AVP) activity. The vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist tolvaptan has been shown to slow TKV growth and GFR decline, and is now FDA-approved for patients with ADPKD over 18 years of age [57]. Given the potential for side effects such as aquaresis and liver dysfunction [57], and the burden of potentially taking a medication for decades, tolvaptan is only recommended for adults at risk for rapid ADPKD progression [66]. The latter procedure should be reserved for patients with severe symptomatic PLD and a liver anatomy that allows the preservation of at least two contiguous liver segments with adequate hepatic venous drainage229. Transcatheter arterial embolization results in only a modest volume reduction and is associated with substantial morbidity and mortality230,231. Liver transplantation is considered for patients when partial liver resection is not feasible. Somatostatin analogues, such as octreotide and lanreotide, can reduce liver cyst volume232–236. Liver cyst infections are a common complication of PLD and are diagnosed, evaluated and managed similarly to infected kidney cysts. Diverticular disease and ascending cholangitis should be considered as a possible source of liver cyst infections. The presentation of ascending cholangitis is characterized by cholestasis with elevation of conjugated bilirubin. Some patients may experience recurrent episodes of cholangitis that may be difficult to treat, sometimes requiring monthly rotating antibiotics to prevent recurrences. Endoscopic sphincterotomy may increase the risk of recurrent cholangitis and should be avoided if possible. It is important to recognize that mild dilatation of the extrahepatic common bile duct can be observed in up to 40% of patients with ADPKD and should not lead to an unnecessary and dangerous sphincterotomy237. question: Is PKD always inherited, or can it just happen randomly? Both my parents seem healthy, so I'm confused about where this came from.
gold_answer:
That's a very understandable source of confusion, and the short answer is: PKD is fundamentally a genetic disease, but it does not always have to be inherited from a parent who is visibly affected. Let me explain the several ways this can happen.

**Most cases are inherited.** Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is typically caused by inherited mutations in the PKD1 or PKD2 genes [P2]. In the classic pattern, one parent carries the mutation and passes it to the child. However, both of your parents appearing healthy does not rule out inheritance, because the disease can be quite variable in severity.

**De novo (new) mutations can occur.** In a meaningful proportion of ADPKD families, the mutation arises spontaneously — it is not inherited from either parent. In approximately 7% of ADPKD families, no mutation is detected by standard testing, and some of these are likely de novo mutations or mosaic cases that are more common than currently appreciated [P6]. So yes, PKD can effectively "just happen" through a new genetic mutation that was not present in either parent.

**Mosaicism is an important and underrecognized mechanism.** Mosaicism occurs when a de novo mutation happens early in embryonic development, so that only some of the person's cells carry the mutation. A study of 20 ADPKD families with mosaicism found that all had PKD1 mutations; in 15 of the 20 families, the cases were sporadic (meaning neither parent was affected), while in 5 families the variant was transmitted to the next generation [P4]. Because mosaic mutations can be difficult to detect by conventional genetic testing, many cases with a negative family history may actually be explained by mosaicism [P4][P6].

**A parent could be an undetected mosaic carrier.** In some cases, a parent may carry a mosaic mutation at such a low level that they have no cysts on imaging and appear completely healthy, yet they can still pass the full mutation to their children through their germ cells. Even germline-only mosaicism is possible, where unaffected parents (with no cysts on imaging) can have more than one affected offspring [P3]. Although the probability of this is low, it should be considered when evaluating a family where the parents seem healthy [P3].

**Rarely, both parents can be silent carriers of mild (hypomorphic) mutations.** In one reported family, both parents carried the same very mild PKD1 mutation in heterozygosity and had completely normal kidneys with no evidence of PKD. Their child, who inherited the mutation from both parents (homozygous), developed polycystic kidney disease through a recessive inheritance pattern [P1]. This is extremely rare but demonstrates another mechanism by which apparently healthy parents can have a child with PKD.

**In summary:** While PKD is a genetic disease, it does not always require a parent with obvious kidney disease. Your situation — having apparently healthy parents — can be explained by a de novo mutation that arose spontaneously in you, mosaicism in one of your parents that is too subtle to cause visible disease, or rarely, both parents carrying very mild mutations that only cause disease when combined. Genetic testing, including next-generation sequencing methods capable of detecting mosaicism, can help clarify the origin of the mutation in your case [P4][P6]. I would recommend discussing genetic counseling and testing options with your care team.

This study represents landmark evidence of the potential for multiple mechanisms of cyst formation in response to mutations in the PKD1 gene. Indeed, up to this report, prior evidence had all supported the supposition that homozygous mutations in the PKD1 gene are not compatible with life. It should be noted that this specific family suffered from a disease that, based on symptoms, should not have been classified as ADPKD, particularly because the cysts in these kidneys appeared as a continuous dilatation of the renal tubule, more similar to the manifestation of recessive PKD. While this case was the only one reported to date and therefore this type of patient appears to be extremely rare, the fact that the parents presented with completely normal kidneys and no evidence of PKD does raise a question regarding the two-hit model of the disease. Indeed, if the combination of two extremely mild mutations in homozygosity is sufficient to cause cyst formation, why do the parents that were carrying the same variant in heterozygosity not present with cyst formation if the somatic mutations in the PKD1 gene occur stochastically and very frequently? question: How does PKD affect cellular metabolism? My doctor mentioned something about the cells in my cysts behaving almost like cancer cells in how they use energy.
gold_answer:
Your doctor is right — the way cyst-lining cells handle energy in PKD has a lot in common with how cancer cells behave, a phenomenon scientists call the **Warburg effect**. Here is what is happening at the cellular level:

**1. A shift from normal energy production to aerobic glycolysis (the Warburg effect)**
In healthy kidney cells, glucose is broken down through glycolysis into pyruvate, which then enters the mitochondria, feeds into the TCA (Krebs) cycle, and generates large amounts of ATP through oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) [P6]. In PKD, however, cells with mutations in PKD1 or PKD2 shift away from this efficient process. Instead, they rely heavily on glycolysis — even when oxygen is available — much like cancer cells do. This is called the Warburg effect and was in fact one of the early metabolic parallels drawn between ADPKD and tumor biology [P5]. The result is increased aerobic glycolysis and extracellular lactate accumulation, with reduced OXPHOS in the mitochondria [P3].

**2. Reduced oxidative phosphorylation and mitochondrial dysfunction**
PKD1 and PKD2 mutations directly impair mitochondrial function, including mitochondrial dynamics, OXPHOS, and fatty acid oxidation (FAO) [P3]. In cystic cells, the normal mitochondrial pathways that produce most of a cell's energy are suppressed, and the cells become reliant on less efficient glycolysis [P2]. Additionally, reduced activity of regulators like PPARγ and PGC1α — which normally promote mitochondrial biogenesis — may worsen the cystic phenotype [P2].

**3. Why do cyst cells use glycolysis like cancer cells?**
Increased glycolysis is commonly seen in rapidly proliferating cells, including cancer cells, because it not only generates ATP but also efficiently converts glucose into the building blocks (macromolecules) needed to construct new cellular components for dividing cells [P5]. Studies have shown that key glycolytic enzymes such as hexokinase 1 (HK1) and hexokinase 2 (HK2) are upregulated in human ADPKD cells [P5]. When PKD1-deficient cells were starved of glucose, they showed decreased proliferation, increased cell death, and defective autophagy — confirming their dependence on this glycolytic pathway [P5].

**4. Other metabolic changes**
The metabolic reprogramming in PKD goes beyond glycolysis. There is also:
- **Enhanced glutaminolysis** — cells increasingly break down the amino acid glutamine as a compensatory energy source [P1].
- **Defective fatty acid β-oxidation** — accumulation of malonyl-CoA inhibits the normal burning of fatty acids for energy [P1][P3].
- **Dysregulated AMPK and mTOR signaling** — reduced AMPK activity and increased mTORC1 activity further drive cell proliferation and growth, compounding the metabolic problem [P3][P4].

**5. Therapeutic implications**
Because of these metabolic similarities to cancer, researchers are exploring strategies that target this abnormal metabolism. Ketogenic dietary interventions (such as caloric restriction, intermittent fasting, and ketogenic diets) can activate AMPK pathways, improve cellular metabolism, and have been shown to reduce cyst growth in preclinical models [P3]. The drug metformin, which also modulates energy metabolism, has been noted as a potential intervention [P2]. Overall, the shift from aerobic (oxidative) to more anaerobic-like (glycolytic) energy pathways in cystic cells represents a promising target for future therapies [P2].

In summary, your doctor's comparison is well supported by research: PKD cyst cells rewire their energy metabolism in ways that closely resemble the Warburg effect seen in cancer, favoring rapid but inefficient glycolysis over normal mitochondrial energy production, and this metabolic shift both fuels cyst growth and opens the door to metabolism-targeted treatments.

question: I've heard that researchers are looking at the actual metabolic changes inside polycystic kidneys to understand the disease better. But how reliable are those studies? Can things like how quickly a tissue sample is handled after removal affect the results?
gold_answer:
Yes, researchers are indeed studying the metabolic changes inside polycystic kidneys to better understand the disease, and your concern about the reliability of those studies is very well-founded. The mammalian kidney has marked intrinsic metabolic activity and high energy demands, consuming the second-highest amount of oxygen per gram of tissue [P3]. Growing evidence suggests that abnormalities in distinct metabolic pathways are implicated in the pathogenesis and progression of many forms of chronic kidney disease (CKD), including polycystic kidney disease (PKD) [P3]. Metabolomics analyses can be used to determine organ-specific metabolic fingerprints and ideally should represent the metabolic state of the organ at the exact moment the sample is harvested [P1].

However, how quickly a tissue sample is handled after removal — the so-called 'harvesting-freezing delay' — can significantly affect the reliability of these studies. Conventional harvesting methods depend on post-euthanasia tissue harvest, which results in ischemia conditions and metabolome changes that could potentially introduce artifacts into the final studies [P1]. Indicators of kidney ischemia include the depletion of high-energy purines such as ATP and ADP, and the accumulation of nucleosides and other metabolites like inosine and hypoxanthine [P2]. Even a short harvesting-freezing delay is sufficient to impart profound metabolic changes in both wild-type (WT) and PKD kidneys [P3].

Importantly, this delay does not affect all samples equally. While the delay had a similar overall effect between WT and PKD kidneys, there were notable differences in how the two genotypes responded [P3]. This means it is not safe to assume that equally delaying harvesting and freezing in wild-type and PKD kidneys adequately controls for this effect [P3]. The consequence is that comparing metabolic profiles between healthy and diseased kidneys can lead to false positive results (detecting differences that don't truly exist in vivo) and false negative results (missing real differences) [P3]. For example, certain metabolites like citrate and alpha-ketoglutarate that appear to accumulate in PKD could be influenced by ischemia-related artifacts during sample handling [P6].

To address this problem, researchers at Mayo Clinic optimized a modified clamp-freezing technique for rodent kidney harvesting that significantly reduces ischemia and freezing times, granting a closer snapshot of in vivo metabolism [P1]. When they compared 49 metabolites between clamp-frozen and conventionally snap-frozen kidneys, 13 metabolites showed significant changes (greater than 1.5-fold change) due to the delay alone [P5]. Their data indicate that this quick clamp-freezing technique provides a more accurate interpretation of the in vivo metabolic changes associated with the disease state [P3].

So in summary, while metabolomics is a powerful tool for understanding PKD, the way tissue samples are handled can substantially alter the results. Immediate freezing of samples is crucial for preserving metabolite integrity [P2], and researchers need to be aware that harvesting delays can drastically alter metabolic profiles, potentially impacting functional interpretations of kidney health and disease [P2]. This is an active area of methodological improvement that should make future metabolic studies of PKD more reliable.

question: What's the difference between the two types of PKD — I heard there's a dominant and a recessive kind? How are they different?
gold_answer:
Great question — yes, there are two main types of polycystic kidney disease (PKD), and while they share some features, they differ in important ways.

**What they have in common:** Both ADPKD and ARPKD cause large, cystic kidneys and both are classified as "ciliopathies" — diseases linked to defects in tiny antenna-like structures on cells called primary cilia [P1]. They also share some common disease pathways [P1]. Both lead to progressive kidney function decline and have distinct liver involvement [P6].

**Autosomal Dominant PKD (ADPKD):**
- **How common:** ADPKD is the most common hereditary kidney disease, occurring in roughly 1 in 1,000 people [P3].
- **Inheritance:** You only need to inherit one mutated copy of the gene (from one parent) to develop the disease [P1].
- **Genes involved:** It is mainly caused by mutations in PKD1 (about 79–85% of cases) or PKD2 (about 15% of cases), which encode the proteins polycystin-1 and polycystin-2 [P3][P5]. Mutations in at least six other minor genes can also cause it [P3]. Patients with PKD1 mutations tend to have more aggressive disease, while PKD2 mutations tend to be somewhat milder, though clinically they look very similar [P1][P5].
- **When it appears:** ADPKD is typically adult-onset [P3][P4], though the range of severity is wider than once recognized [P6].
- **Kidney features:** Cysts form as outpouchings of the kidney tubules throughout all tubular segments [P5]. Cysts appear predominantly in the distal convoluted tubules and collecting ducts [P4].
- **Other organ involvement:** Hypertension is very common (20–40% in children/adolescents, 50–70% in adults before kidney function declines) [P4]. Liver cysts are common and can sometimes cause significant polycystic liver disease [P3]. There is also an increased risk of intracranial (brain) aneurysms, occurring in about 8% of patients with a threefold increase in those with a family history [P4][P3]. About 50% of patients eventually reach end-stage kidney disease [P4].

**Autosomal Recessive PKD (ARPKD):**
- **How common:** ARPKD is much rarer, occurring in roughly 1 in 20,000 people [P3].
- **Inheritance:** A child must inherit two mutated copies of the gene (one from each parent) to develop the disease [P2].
- **Gene involved:** It is mainly caused by mutations in a single gene called PKHD1, located on chromosome 6, which encodes the protein fibrocystin (also called polyductin) [P2][P3].
- **When it appears:** ARPKD typically presents much earlier — often before birth or in the newborn period, with massively enlarged cystic kidneys detectable in utero or perinatally [P3]. It leads to death in the newborn period in approximately 25% of affected babies [P3]. However, some patients present later, even as adults [P3].
- **Other organ involvement:** Rather than liver cysts (as seen in ADPKD), ARPKD is characterized by congenital hepatic fibrosis (CHF) — scarring of the liver [P3]. In some patients who present later, the liver fibrosis may actually be the more prominent feature [P3].

**Molecular connections:** Interestingly, the proteins made by the ADPKD genes (polycystin-1 and polycystin-2) and the ARPKD gene (fibrocystin) can interact with each other — they "cross talk" both genetically and at the protein level, and all localize to primary cilia [P2][P5]. This helps explain why both diseases result in kidney cysts despite being caused by different genes.

In summary, ADPKD is common, requires only one mutated gene copy, usually shows up in adulthood, and is associated with liver cysts and brain aneurysms. ARPKD is rare, requires two mutated gene copies, often presents before or shortly after birth with a more severe course, and is associated with liver fibrosis rather than liver cysts [P1][P3][P6].

They are caused by inherited mutations in different genes, manifest at different time points and in different regions of the renal tubule, and they show quite different extrarenal manifestations. However, they both belong to the family of genetic diseases known as “ciliopapthies” and share common pathogenetic pathways that likely intersect at multiple levels. i. The two genes were cloned in 1994 and 1996, respectively, through classical positional cloning efforts. These diseases also have distinct liver manifestations. The range of clinical presentation and severity of both ADPKD and ARPKD is much wider than was once recognized. question: Does the complement system in my immune system play any role in cyst growth? My doctor briefly mentioned inflammation being involved.
gold_answer:
Yes, your doctor is right — emerging research strongly suggests that the complement system, a part of your innate immune system, plays an active role in driving cyst growth in ADPKD, and it does so in close connection with inflammation.

**Complement activation in ADPKD kidneys:** Complement components (such as C3b) have been found deposited on kidney tubular cells in ADPKD, and complement split products have been detected in the urine and cystic fluid of ADPKD patients [P5][P6]. Importantly, kidney cells that lack functional polycystin-1 (the protein encoded by the PKD1 gene) show increased expression of complement-related genes, including C3, C5, complement factor B (CfB), and the receptors for complement fragments C3a and C5a [P2].

**Why is complement overactivated?** Normally, complement activation is kept in check by protective molecules on cell surfaces, such as decay-accelerating factor (DAF). In ADPKD, loss of PKD1 function leads to significantly reduced DAF expression, which removes an important brake on complement activation and allows it to proceed unchecked on tubular cells [P6][P3].

**Direct evidence that complement drives cyst growth:** In mouse models, genetically deleting C3 — the central molecule of the complement cascade — significantly slowed cyst development in Pkd1-deficient mice [P1]. This is a critical finding because it shows that complement activation is not merely a bystander effect of tissue damage; rather, it is actively fueling cyst growth [P1]. Additionally, a nonspecific complement inhibitor called rosmarinic acid has been shown to improve disease severity in ADPKD mouse models, further supporting this conclusion [P1].

**The link to inflammation:** The complement system and inflammation are tightly interconnected in ADPKD. ADPKD kidneys exist in a state of chronic injury and inflammation due to progressive cyst expansion and tissue remodeling [P5]. Complement fragments like C5a act as chemoattractants that recruit immune cells. Specifically, mice lacking C3 (and thus unable to generate C3a, C5a, or the membrane attack complex) showed reduced inflammatory cytokine expression and fewer macrophage infiltrates in their kidneys [P1][P3]. Macrophage infiltration (detected by the F4/80 marker) was significantly higher in Pkd1-deficient kidneys compared to normal kidneys, and this was reduced when C3 was also deleted [P4]. The signaling of C5a through its receptor C5aR1 on tubular cells appears to be a key mechanism promoting proliferation of PKD1-deficient cells [P3].

**The proposed mechanism:** The current understanding is that in normal kidneys, complement activation is part of a controlled injury-repair response. However, when polycystin-1 is absent or dysfunctional, this process becomes uncontrolled — complement is overproduced, its regulators (like DAF) are reduced, and the resulting complement fragments drive both direct tubular cell proliferation and recruitment of inflammatory cells (especially macrophages), creating a vicious cycle that promotes cystogenesis [P1][P3][P6].

So in summary, complement activation is now recognized not just as a consequence of cyst-related tissue damage, but as an active driver of cyst growth, and it works hand-in-hand with inflammation to worsen the disease. This understanding opens up potential new therapeutic targets for ADPKD.

Therefore, these findings indicate that the deposition of complement split products in ADPKD kidneys and the presence of complement components in cystic fluid (5, 23, 24) are not a mere downstream effect of progressive kidney tissue destruction and inflammation, but a key element fueling cyst growth. question: I've heard PKD is caused by mutations in specific genes. What do those genes normally do, and what goes wrong when they're mutated?
gold_answer:
Polycystic kidney disease (PKD) comes in two main forms, each caused by mutations in different genes:

**Autosomal Dominant PKD (ADPKD):**
ADPKD is most commonly caused by mutations in two genes: **PKD1** (on chromosome 16p13.3), which accounts for about 80–85% of cases, and **PKD2** (on chromosome 4q22.1), which accounts for roughly 15% of cases [P1][P3][P5]. Patients with PKD1 mutations tend to have a more aggressive disease course, while those with PKD2 mutations generally experience somewhat milder progression, though the two forms are clinically indistinguishable [P1].

These genes encode proteins called **polycystin-1 (PC1)** and **polycystin-2 (PC2)**, respectively [P3][P4]. Under normal circumstances:

- **PC1** is a large integral membrane receptor protein with a big extracellular region containing domains involved in **cell-cell and cell-matrix interactions**, 11 transmembrane domains, and a short cytoplasmic segment with motifs important for **signal transduction** [P3].
- **PC2** functions as part of a calcium signaling system. Both PC1 and PC2 are localized to multiple cellular compartments, including the **primary cilia**, the **plasma membrane**, and the **endoplasmic reticulum (ER)** [P3][P6]. PKD is considered a "ciliopathy" because these proteins are localized to cilia and their dysfunction disrupts ciliary signaling [P1][P6].

**What goes wrong when these genes are mutated:**
Cyst development appears to follow a "two-hit" model: a person inherits one mutated copy of PKD1 or PKD2 (germline mutation), and then a somatic "second hit" — such as loss of heterozygosity or additional mutations — occurs in individual kidney cells, triggering cyst formation [P3]. When polycystin function is lost, **calcium signaling is disrupted**. Specifically, PC1 or PC2 loss of function on the primary cilia and ER impairs the normal calcium transient response, which leads to **intracellular cAMP accumulation** [P4]. This dysregulation of calcium signaling causes the **aberrant activation of pathways associated with abnormal cell proliferation and fluid secretion**, which drives the development and progressive expansion of kidney cysts [P3][P4]. As cysts enlarge, they compress neighboring healthy kidney tissue and progressively decrease filtration function [P4].

A remaining ~5–10% of ADPKD cases are caused by rare mutations in other genes, including HNF1B, GANAB, DNAJB11, SEC63, and PRKCSH, many of which are involved in protein folding or translocation across the ER membrane [P5].

**Autosomal Recessive PKD (ARPKD):**
ARPKD is primarily caused by mutations in **PKHD1**, which encodes a protein called **fibrocystin**, and in **DZIP1L**, which encodes DAZ-interacting protein 1-like protein [P4][P6]. ARPKD tends to be more aggressive, often manifesting in the perinatal period or early childhood [P6]. Fibrocystin, like the polycystins, is localized to cilia, reinforcing that PKD is fundamentally a ciliary disease [P6].

In summary, the PKD genes normally produce proteins critical for calcium signaling, cell-cell communication, and maintaining proper cell growth and fluid balance in kidney tubules. When mutated, these functions are lost, leading to uncontrolled cell proliferation, abnormal fluid secretion, and the progressive formation and enlargement of cysts that ultimately destroy kidney function [P3][P4][P6].

Autosomal-dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is a form of PKD, which is a result of mutations in PKD1 or PKD2 that encode polycystin1 (PC1) or polycystin2 (PC2), respectively. In ADPKD patients, cysts originate in utero and later form in the collecting ducts and distal part of the nephron. Extrarenal manifestations in these patients include hepatobiliary fibrosis, liver cysts, hypertension, and intracranial arterial aneurysms. Autosomal-recessive PKD (ARPKD) is a rare form of PKD, which is primarily caused by mutations in polycystic kidney and hepatic disease 1 (PKHD1) mutations, which encode fibrocystin. ARPKD is more aggressive and starts at the perinatal period or early childhood. The mechanisms of cyst initiation are not well understood, but it is known that cyst growth is driven by epithelial cell proliferation, fluid secretion, and changes to the microenvironment. PKD is also thought to be a ciliary disease as PC1, PC2, and fibrocystin are localized to cilia. Moreover, many ciliary gene mutations (MCKD2, NPHP3) are associated with medullary and glomerular cysts in the kidneys [1]. Tolvaptan is the only drug targeted to slow down the progression of cysts in ADPKD patients, which itself has hepatotoxic effects. Thus, understanding the molecular pathways involved in cyst formation and progression are important to develop effective and safe drugs. Notch Correspondence: Madhulika Sharma

question: Can PKD also affect my liver or other organs, or is it just a kidney problem?
gold_answer:
PKD is not just a kidney problem — it is a systemic disease that can affect several other organs, most notably the liver.

**Liver involvement:**
In autosomal dominant PKD (ADPKD), liver cysts are common and sometimes result in clinically significant polycystic liver disease (PLD) [P3]. PLD is characterized by the presence of multiple cysts scattered throughout the liver parenchyma, which form owing to overgrowth of the biliary epithelium, and can range from a few hepatic cysts to a massively enlarged cystic liver [P5]. In fact, hepatomegaly and increased liver volume can occur even before liver cysts are detected by imaging, possibly due to microscopic cysts, biliary epithelial proliferation, or hepatocyte hyperplasia [P4]. ADPKD is described as a systemic disorder associated with numerous extrarenal manifestations, many of which arise in the gastrointestinal tract [P4]. Women with truncating PKD1 mutations may have a more severe liver phenotype [P6].

In autosomal recessive PKD (ARPKD), liver involvement can include biliary duct dilatation, congenital hepatic fibrosis (CHF), and portal hypertension [P1]. Severe liver disease manifestations in ARPKD can include recurrent ascending cholangitis or refractory variceal bleeding, and may ultimately necessitate portosystemic shunt placement and/or liver transplantation [P1]. Some ARPKD patients present as adults with more prominent CHF [P3].

**Cardiovascular involvement:**
Patients with ADPKD also have cardiovascular abnormalities, including hypertension and left ventricular hypertrophy [P5].

**Intracranial aneurysms:**
There is an increased predisposition to intracranial aneurysms in ADPKD [P3].

**Syndromic forms:**
Beyond ADPKD and ARPKD, there are syndromic forms of PKD that affect numerous organ systems, including Meckel syndrome, Joubert syndrome, nephronophthisis, and Bardet-Biedl syndrome [P3].

In summary, PKD — especially ADPKD — is far more than a kidney disease. The liver is the most commonly affected extrarenal organ, and cardiovascular complications and intracranial aneurysms are also recognized features of the disease.

However, the clinical expression of liver disease also varies widely between patients. Although all patients have the developmental duct abnormality termed ductal plate malformation, some patients may remain asymptomatic while others may have severe consequences. Manifestations of severe liver disease include recurrent ascending cholangitis or refractory variceal bleeding, and ultimately may necessitate portosystemic shunt placement and/or liver transplantation [102]. Predictors of kidney disease progression in ARPKD Genetic predictors—ARPKD is most commonly caused by mutations in PKHD1, a very large gene that encodes a transmembrane protein called fibrocystin/polyductin that localizes to the primary cilium [103]. A large number of pathogenic variants in PKHD1 can cause ARPKD, including missense, truncating, and intronic/splice-site mutations. The most common mutation, c.107C>T (T36M), accounts for about 15 to 20% of mutant alleles and is sometimes called a “mutational hotspot” [104]. Aside from the T36M mutation, there is no other evidence of clustering mutations at specific sites in the PKHD1 gene. question: My doctor said something about a protein called polycystin. What does that protein actually do, and how does it relate to cyst formation?
gold_answer:
Polycystin refers to two related proteins—Polycystin-1 (PC-1) and Polycystin-2 (PC-2)—that are encoded by the PKD1 and PKD2 genes, respectively. Here is what we know about their structure, function, and role in cyst formation:

**Structure:**
Polycystin-1 is a very large transmembrane protein consisting of 4,302 amino acids with a predicted molecular weight of ~462 kDa. Its large extracellular region contains multiple functional domains, including leucine-rich repeats (LRR), immunoglobulin-like PKD repeats, and a Receptor for Egg Jelly (REJ) region, among others [P2]. PC-1 and PC-2 physically interact to form a heterotetrameric complex made up of one molecule of PC-1 and three molecules of PC-2, in which the proteins are elaborately intertwined through their transmembrane helices [P3].

**Where they are found:**
The polycystin proteins are located at multiple cellular sites, including the plasma membrane, adherens junctions, desmosomes, focal adhesions, and—importantly—the primary cilium. They are also secreted in microvesicles. PC-2 additionally resides in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), where it mediates calcium (Ca²⁺) release [P4]. The PC-1–PC-2 complex has also been found at mitochondria-associated membranes, where it can directly regulate oxidative phosphorylation by mediating mitochondrial calcium uptake [P5].

**What they do:**
The polycystin proteins form multimeric complexes that modulate numerous signaling pathways, including Ca²⁺ signaling, cAMP, mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin), WNT, VEGF, and Hippo signaling [P4]. They influence a broad range of essential cellular functions such as fluid transport, proliferation, apoptosis, cell adhesion, and differentiation [P4]. Despite over 20 years of research, their precise normal cellular roles are still not fully understood [P1].

**How loss of polycystin leads to cyst formation:**
When the polycystin proteins are absent or dysfunctional—due to mutations in PKD1 or PKD2—a cascade of cellular changes occurs. Key downstream effects include reduced Ca²⁺ influx, increased cAMP levels, and aberrant activation of the RAS–RAF–ERK pathway, all of which are important mediators of cyst growth [P4]. These disruptions drive the proliferation of cyst-lining epithelial cells and active transepithelial fluid secretion into the cyst lumen, which are the two main processes that cause cysts to expand [P5]. Alterations in cellular metabolism—such as increased glucose consumption through aerobic glycolysis and impaired fatty acid oxidation—are additional features accompanying cyst expansion [P6]. Furthermore, growing cysts can impose mechanical stress on neighboring nephrons, causing apoptosis of adjacent epithelial cells and locally increasing the likelihood of new cyst formation [P6].

Importantly, it is the reduction in levels of functional polycystin proteins that drives cystic disease: complete loss of PC-1 or PC-2 exacerbates cyst development, whereas modest overexpression of these proteins from transgenes can suppress the disease [P3]. This indicates that maintaining adequate polycystin function is critical to preventing cyst formation.

In summary, the polycystin proteins are complex signaling molecules that regulate calcium handling, cell growth, fluid secretion, and multiple other pathways. When they are lost or impaired, the resulting signaling imbalances lead to uncontrolled epithelial cell proliferation and fluid accumulation, which are the hallmarks of cyst formation in PKD.

question: I keep hearing about how cAMP drives cyst growth. Can someone explain what cAMP is and why it matters in PKD in plain terms?
gold_answer:
Great question! Let me break this down in plain language.

**What is cAMP?**
cAMP (cyclic AMP) is a tiny signaling molecule inside your cells — think of it as a chemical "messenger" that tells cells what to do. It's made from ATP (the cell's energy currency) by an enzyme called adenylyl cyclase, and it's broken down by other enzymes called phosphodiesterases (PDEs) [P2]. cAMP is important for many normal cellular processes, including cell growth, DNA synthesis, and cell differentiation [P2].

**Why does cAMP matter in PKD?**
In PKD, cAMP levels become abnormally high inside the cells that line kidney cysts [P3]. This happens because the polycystin proteins (which are faulty or missing in PKD) normally help keep cAMP in check. When polycystins don't work properly, adenylate cyclase activity goes up and phosphodiesterase activity goes down, leading to a buildup of cAMP [P1]. There may also be a "feed-forward" loop where cAMP activates a transcription factor called CREB, which increases the expression of a kinase called GSK3β, which in turn further boosts adenylate cyclase activity — creating a vicious cycle of ever-rising cAMP [P4]. Additionally, the loss of primary cilia function (which is linked to polycystin problems) further activates adenylyl cyclase and raises cAMP levels [P2].

**How does elevated cAMP drive cyst growth?**
Elevated cAMP does two harmful things in PKD:

1. **It makes cyst-lining cells multiply too fast.** cAMP stimulates cell proliferation through signaling pathways called B-Raf/MEK/ERK [P2][P4]. This is partly connected to the reduced levels of calcium inside PKD cells, which seems to "switch" cAMP's effect from normal to pro-growth [P4].

2. **It drives fluid into cysts, making them balloon larger.** cAMP activates chloride secretion through a channel called CFTR, which pulls water into the cyst lumen, inflating the cysts [P1][P2].

So in simple terms: too much cAMP = too much cell growth + too much fluid pumped into cysts = cysts that keep getting bigger [P6].

**Why this matters for treatment:**
Because cAMP is so central to cyst growth, reducing cAMP levels is one of the most promising treatment strategies [P1]. The drug tolvaptan works by blocking the vasopressin V2 receptor in collecting duct cells, which is a major driver of cAMP production in the kidney [P5]. Vasopressin (the anti-diuretic hormone) normally stimulates cAMP production in these cells, so blocking its receptor lowers cAMP and slows cyst development [P5]. Tolvaptan is currently the only FDA-approved treatment for ADPKD and has been shown to slow cyst growth and preserve kidney function [P5]. Other approaches to lowering cAMP — such as activating the somatostatin receptor or the calcium-sensing receptor (with drugs like cinacalcet) — are also being explored [P5][P6]. Another natural protective mechanism involves the enzyme AMPK, which can activate phosphodiesterases to break down cAMP, but AMPK activity is unfortunately reduced in PKD cells [P3].

question: I was told PKD is genetic. Does that mean all my children will definitely get it, or is there a chance they won't inherit it?
gold_answer:
Having PKD does not mean all your children will definitely inherit the condition — the chance depends on the type of PKD.

The most common form, autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), is caused by mutations in the PKD1 or PKD2 genes [P2]. Because it follows autosomal dominant inheritance, each child of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting the mutation and a 50% chance of not inheriting it. So some of your children may be unaffected.

It is also worth knowing that even among family members who carry the same mutation, the severity of the disease can vary considerably. For example, patients with PKD1 mutations tend to have more aggressive disease (median age of kidney failure around 58 years), while those with PKD2 mutations tend to have milder progression (median age of kidney failure around 79 years) [P6]. Additional genetic factors — such as co-inheritance of other cystic kidney disease gene mutations (e.g., HNF1B or PKHD1) or hypomorphic alleles — can also modify disease severity [P4].

In a small number of cases (about 2–5%), ADPKD can arise from de novo (new) mutations, meaning there is no prior family history at all [P6]. In very rare instances, two unaffected parents each carrying a mild (hypomorphic) PKD1 variant can have a child with PKD when the child inherits both copies [P3][P4].

The other major form, autosomal recessive PKD (ARPKD), follows a different pattern: both parents must carry a mutation in the PKHD1 gene for a child to be affected, and each pregnancy carries a 25% chance the child will have the disease. If your partner is unaffected by ARPKD and has no family history of PKD, the risk for your offspring to have ARPKD is very low (less than 1%) [P1].

In summary, PKD is indeed genetic, but it does not mean every child will be affected. With ADPKD, each child has roughly a 50-50 chance of inheriting the mutation. Genetic counseling and, when appropriate, molecular genetic testing can help clarify the risk for your specific family [P1][P5].

Other relatives (such as healthy siblings) or the affected patients themselves may seek genetic counselling and can usually be reassured of a very low risk (<1%) for ARPKD for their own offspring if their partner is not affected by ARPKD and has no family history of PKD. Prenatal screening.—Many parents of children with ARPKD seek early and reliable prenatal diagnosis of subsequent children to guide future family planning. Frequently, patients with ARPKD are diagnosed by ultrasonography only late in pregnancy or at birth. However, fetal sonography at the time when pregnancies are usually terminated may fail to detect enlargement and increased echogenicity of the kidneys or oligohydramnios (deficiency of amniotic fluid) secondary to poor fetal urine output. Thus, an early and reliable prenatal diagnosis of ARPKD in at-risk families is feasible only by molecular genetic analysis. Prenatal diagnosis should be offered only to families for which there is an unequivocal diagnosis in an affected family member. However, prenatal molecular genetic analysis is usually done using chorionic villus sampling, which is an invasive procedure that is carried out quite late in pregnancy (no earlier than week 10–12 of gestation). Besides sharing the key manifestation of developing large and polycystic kidneys, the two disorders are very different (Figure 1). They are caused by inherited mutations in different genes, manifest at different time points and in different regions of the renal tubule, and they show quite different extrarenal manifestations. The two genes were cloned in 1994 and 1996, respectively, through classical positional cloning efforts. Occasionally, ADPKD can present prenatally or in infancy with a phenotype that resembles the classic presentation of ARPKD, a condition known as very early onset (VEO)-ADPKD. Some cases of VEO-ADPKD can be explained by coexistence of multiple PKD mutant alleles. Reported examples include coinheritance of an inactivating PKD1 allele from an affected parent and an incompletely penetrant or hypomorphic PKD1 allele from an unaffected parent [14-16], or coinheritance of two hypomorphic PKD1 alleles from unaffected or mildly affected parents [15, 17]. These findings suggest that the “dosage” of functional polycystin protein affects the severity of disease expression [15]. Co-inheritance of PKD1 or PKD2 mutations with other kidney cystic gene mutations such as HNF1B and PKHD1 is also associated with more severe ADPKD phenotypes that can resemble ARPKD [14]. In these cases, genetic testing using next-generation sequencing panels to test multiple cystic kidney disease genes can be helpful to verify the diagnosis and provide guidance for disease management in childhood. With growing recognition of clinical manifestations of ADPKD in childhood, pediatric nephrologists are increasingly becoming responsible for the care of patients with ADPKD. Patients with ADPKD can also have liver cysts, with clinical expression that can range from no or few asymptomatic cysts to severe and debilitating polycystic liver disease requiring surgical intervention [6]. Predictors of kidney disease progression in ADPKD Genetic predictors—ADPKD is most commonly caused by mutations in PKD1 or PKD2. Although the majority of ADPKD patients have a positive family history, 2-5% of patients may have de novo mutations [7]. Mutations in two additional genes, GANAB and DNAJB11, were recently also described in several pedigrees with atypical ADPKD phenotypes [8, 9]. PKD1 encodes polycystin-1 (PC1), a large glycoprotein, and PKD2 encodes polycystin-2 (PC2), a calcium-regulated cation channel [10]. PC1 and PC2 have been found to localize to the primary cilia (among other subcellular locations), where they interact with and are functionally dependent on one another [11]. The most notable genotypic predictor of ADPKD progression is the presence of PKD1 versus PKD2 mutations. question: I've heard PKD can also cause cysts in the liver. How common is that, and does it cause problems the way kidney cysts do?
gold_answer:
Liver cysts are very common in ADPKD. They are eventually seen in approximately 90% of people with ADPKD, and their prevalence increases with age [P6]. Liver cysts in ADPKD typically develop later than kidney cysts and tend to be more numerous and larger in women than in men [P6].

However, liver cysts generally do not cause the same kind of problems that kidney cysts do. Unlike kidney cysts, which progressively enlarge the kidneys and reduce their function — leading to end-stage renal disease in roughly 50% of patients by the fifth to sixth decade of life [P2] — liver cysts usually do not impair the liver's synthetic or secretory capacity, even in advanced polycystic liver disease (PLD) [P6]. Most people with ADPKD who have liver cysts will not develop clinically symptomatic PLD [P6], and liver cysts typically remain asymptomatic throughout life [P6].

That said, liver cysts can occasionally cause problems related to their mass effect when the liver becomes very large [P6]. The severity of PLD can range from a few hepatic cysts to a massively enlarged cystic liver [P5]. The cysts form due to overgrowth of the biliary epithelium, driven by a substantial reduction or complete loss of function of the polycystin proteins, similar to the mechanism in kidney cysts [P5]. Additionally, ADPKD can be associated with dilated common bile ducts — one study found that 40% of patients with ADPKD had a dilated common bile duct on CT scan compared to just 9% in patients without ADPKD [P1].

In summary, while liver cysts are extremely common in ADPKD, they are usually much less problematic than kidney cysts because they rarely impair liver function, though in severe cases they can cause symptoms from mass effect.

question: Is there an autosomal recessive form of PKD too? How is it different from the dominant kind that most people talk about?
gold_answer:
Yes, there is indeed an autosomal recessive form of polycystic kidney disease (ARPKD), and it differs from the more commonly discussed autosomal dominant form (ADPKD) in several important ways.

**Genetics:** ADPKD is most commonly caused by mutations in two genes, PKD1 (on chromosome 16p13.3) and PKD2 (on chromosome 4q13), which encode the proteins polycystin-1 and polycystin-2, respectively [P1]. PKD1 accounts for approximately 79% and PKD2 for approximately 15% of ADPKD cases, with mutations in at least six other genes (ALG5, ALG8, ALG9, GANAB, DNAJB11, and IFT140) being minor causes [P6]. In contrast, ARPKD is primarily caused by biallelic (two-copy) mutations in the gene PKHD1, located on chromosome 6p12, which encodes a protein called fibrocystin [P4][P6]. Because ARPKD is recessive, a child must inherit a mutated copy from each parent to develop the disease, whereas in ADPKD, inheriting just one mutated copy is typically sufficient [P4][P1].

**Age of onset and severity:** ADPKD typically manifests symptoms in adulthood, with the mean age of developing kidney failure in the 5th through 7th decades of life [P2]. ARPKD is much more aggressive and typically presents in the perinatal period or early childhood [P3]. ARPKD leads to neonatal lethality in approximately 25% of patients and can result in childhood end-stage kidney disease [P6]. The mean age of kidney failure in ARPKD is in the 1st and 2nd decades of life [P2].

**Prevalence:** ADPKD is far more common, with a prevalence of approximately 1 in 1,000 [P6]. ARPKD is much rarer, with an incidence of approximately 1 in 20,000 [P6].

**Extrarenal manifestations:** Both diseases affect the liver, but in different ways. ADPKD is associated with liver cysts, which can sometimes result in clinically significant polycystic liver disease, as well as hypertension and an increased predisposition to intracranial aneurysms [P6][P3]. ARPKD is typically characterized by congenital hepatic fibrosis (CHF) rather than liver cysts, and some patients with ARPKD may present as adults with more prominent CHF [P6].

**Cyst location:** In ADPKD, cysts originate in utero and later form in the collecting ducts and distal part of the nephron [P3]. The two diseases manifest in different regions of the renal tubule [P1].

**Shared features:** Despite these differences, both ADPKD and ARPKD are characterized by bilateral cystic kidney disease leading to progressive kidney function decline [P5]. Both are considered ciliopathies, as polycystin-1, polycystin-2, and fibrocystin are all localized to primary cilia [P3][P6]. The PKD1/2 and PKHD1 genes can cross-talk to each other both genetically and at the protein level, and the diseases share common pathogenetic pathways that likely intersect at multiple levels [P4][P1]. There is also phenotypic heterogeneity in both diseases, with the range of clinical presentation and severity being wider than was once recognized [P5][P6].

Genetics of PKD As mentioned above, PKD presents with two distinct modalities of transmission: either autosomal dominant (ADPKD) or autosomal recessive (ARPKD). Besides sharing the key manifestation of developing large and polycystic kidneys, the two disorders are very different (Figure 1). They are caused by inherited mutations in different genes, manifest at different time points and in different regions of the renal tubule, and they show quite different extrarenal manifestations. However, they both belong to the family of genetic diseases known as “ciliopapthies” and share common pathogenetic pathways that likely intersect at multiple levels. i. PKD1 and 2 genes/polycystins 1 and 2: ADPKD presents with a number of clinical features that allow a diagnosis to be made without performing any molecular testing. The most typical forms of ADPKD are caused by mutations in two genes, PKD1 and PKD2, localized on Chromosome 16p13.3 and on chromosome 4q13 respectively. The two genes were cloned in 1994 and 1996, respectively, through classical positional cloning efforts. Patients inheriting mutations in the PKD1 gene tend to have a more aggressive form of the disease, while patients with inherited PKD2 mutations tend to have on average a somewhat milder disease progression. There are two forms: autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), which typically manifests symptoms in adulthood, and autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease (ARPKD), which typically manifests symptoms in childhood. In children, the overall prevalence of known PKD is estimated to be around 1 in 10,000 [1]. Both ADPKD and ARPKD are characterized by development of cysts resulting in kidney enlargement and progressive chronic kidney disease (CKD), which can ultimately lead to kidney failure. Autosomal-dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is a form of PKD, which is a result of mutations in PKD1 or PKD2 that encode polycystin1 (PC1) or polycystin2 (PC2), respectively. In ADPKD patients, cysts originate in utero and later form in the collecting ducts and distal part of the nephron. Extrarenal manifestations in these patients include hepatobiliary fibrosis, liver cysts, hypertension, and intracranial arterial aneurysms. Autosomal-recessive PKD (ARPKD) is a rare form of PKD, which is primarily caused by mutations in polycystic kidney and hepatic disease 1 (PKHD1) mutations, which encode fibrocystin. ARPKD is more aggressive and starts at the perinatal period or early childhood. The mechanisms of cyst initiation are not well understood, but it is known that cyst growth is driven by epithelial cell proliferation, fluid secretion, and changes to the microenvironment. PKD is also thought to be a ciliary disease as PC1, PC2, and fibrocystin are localized to cilia. Moreover, many ciliary gene mutations (MCKD2, NPHP3) are associated with medullary and glomerular cysts in the kidneys [1]. Tolvaptan is the only drug targeted to slow down the progression of cysts in ADPKD patients, which itself has hepatotoxic effects. Thus, understanding the molecular pathways involved in cyst formation and progression are important to develop effective and safe drugs. Notch Correspondence: Madhulika Sharma

These diseases also have distinct liver manifestations. The range of clinical presentation and severity of both ADPKD and ARPKD is much wider than was once recognized. Pediatric and adult nephrologists are likely to care for individuals with both diseases in their lifetimes. This article will review genetic, clinical, and imaging predictors of kidney and liver disease progression in ADPKD and ARPKD, and will briefly summarize pharmacologic therapies to prevent progression. question: What exactly causes PKD? I just got diagnosed and I'm trying to understand why cysts form in my kidneys.
gold_answer:
I understand this is a lot to take in after a new diagnosis, so let me walk you through the basics of what causes PKD and why cysts form.

**The genetic root cause:**
Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is caused by mutations in one of two genes: **PKD1** or **PKD2**. Mutations in PKD1 account for about 85% of cases, while PKD2 mutations account for the remaining 15% [P1][P6]. These two genes provide the instructions for making proteins called **polycystin-1 (PC1)** and **polycystin-2 (PC2)**, respectively [P1][P4]. PC1 is a large membrane receptor involved in cell-cell and cell-matrix interactions and signal transduction, while PC2 works alongside it in signaling [P1].

**How cysts get started:**
You inherit one mutated copy of PKD1 or PKD2 from the affected parent (a germline mutation). However, cysts don't form in every single kidney cell — they develop focally (in scattered individual cells). This is consistent with what researchers call a "two-hit" model: a second, somatic mutation occurs randomly in the remaining normal copy of the gene within individual kidney cells over the course of your lifetime, and this second hit is what triggers cyst formation in those particular cells [P1][P6]. An alternative (or additional) explanation involves the overall dosage of functional polycystin protein — when the level of working polycystin drops below a critical threshold, cyst formation can be initiated [P6].

**Why cysts grow and enlarge:**
Once a cyst begins, its growth is driven by several key processes:

1. **Abnormal cell proliferation:** The cells lining the cyst multiply excessively instead of behaving like normal, quiescent kidney tubule cells [P2][P4].
2. **Fluid secretion:** Rather than reabsorbing fluid as normal tubule cells do, cyst-lining cells actively secrete fluid into the cyst cavity, inflating it like a balloon [P2][P4].
3. **Disrupted calcium signaling:** Loss of polycystin function impairs normal calcium signaling in the cell. This dysregulation of calcium leads to abnormal accumulation of a molecule called cAMP, which in turn drives both the excessive cell proliferation and fluid secretion that enlarge cysts [P1][P4].
4. **Loss of normal tissue organization:** Cyst cells lose their normal planar polarity (the organized orientation within the tubule), causing cysts to sprout off the tubule axis [P4].
5. **Changes to the microenvironment:** The surrounding tissue environment is also altered, further promoting cyst expansion [P2].

As cysts continue to enlarge over years, they compress and destroy the neighboring healthy kidney tissue, progressively reducing kidney function [P4][P6]. About 50% of patients reach end-stage renal disease by their fifth to sixth decade of life [P6].

**Where in the kidney cysts form:**
In ADPKD, cysts originate from the tubular epithelial cells — specifically in structures like the collecting ducts and distal nephron [P2][P5]. The cysts can begin forming even in utero and continue to develop and grow throughout life [P2].

**The connection to cilia:**
PKD is also considered a "ciliary disease" because PC1, PC2, and related proteins are found on primary cilia — tiny antenna-like structures on kidney cells that sense the environment. Mutations in various ciliary genes are also associated with cystic kidney diseases [P2][P4].

It is important to note that while we understand many of these mechanisms, the complete cascade of events between polycystin dysfunction and cyst formation is still not fully understood [P1][P3]. Researchers continue to study these pathways actively in order to develop better treatments. Currently, tolvaptan is the only drug specifically approved to slow cyst progression in ADPKD [P2].

supporting_passages.text: to speculate about the significance of these interactions. Autosomal-dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is a form of PKD, which is a result of mutations in PKD1 or PKD2 that encode polycystin1 (PC1) or polycystin2 (PC2), respectively. In ADPKD patients, cysts originate in utero and later form in the collecting ducts and distal part of the nephron. Extrarenal manifestations in these patients include hepatobiliary fibrosis, liver cysts, hypertension, and intracranial arterial aneurysms. Autosomal-recessive PKD (ARPKD) is a rare form of PKD, which is primarily caused by mutations in polycystic kidney and hepatic disease 1 (PKHD1) mutations, which encode fibrocystin. ARPKD is more aggressive and starts at the perinatal period or early childhood. The mechanisms of cyst initiation are not well understood, but it is known that cyst growth is driven by epithelial cell proliferation, fluid secretion, and changes to the microenvironment. PKD is also thought to be a ciliary disease as PC1, PC2, and fibrocystin are localized to cilia. Moreover, many ciliary gene mutations (MCKD2, NPHP3) are associated with medullary and glomerular cysts in the kidneys [1]. Tolvaptan is the only drug targeted to slow down the progression of cysts in ADPKD patients, which itself has hepatotoxic effects. Thus, understanding the molecular pathways involved in cyst formation and progression are important to develop effective and safe drugs. Notch Correspondence: Madhulika Sharma

question: What genes are involved in polycystic kidney disease, and does it matter which one is affected?
gold_answer:
Polycystic kidney disease (PKD) involves several genes, but the two major ones are **PKD1** and **PKD2**, which together account for more than 90% of affected families in research studies [P3]. These genes encode the proteins polycystin-1 and polycystin-2, respectively, which are co-localized in renal cilia and work together as a receptor–ion channel complex [P6]. In addition to PKD1 and PKD2, several minor genes with an ADPKD-spectrum phenotype have been described over the past decade, accounting for a small percentage of affected families [P3]. For autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease (ARPKD), the gene PKHD1 is involved [P4].

It matters greatly which gene is affected:

1. **PKD1 vs. PKD2 severity:** Patients with PKD1 mutations generally have a more severe disease course. Males with PKD1 truncating mutations have an earlier age of kidney failure onset than females (53 vs. 58 years), though this sex-based difference was not seen in patients with non-truncating PKD1 mutations [P4].

2. **Type of mutation matters:** The type of PKD1 mutation influences renal outcome in ADPKD [P5]. Truncating mutations in PKD1 are generally associated with more severe disease compared to non-truncating mutations [P4]. The predicted mutation strength of non-truncating PKD1 mutations can aid in genotype–phenotype correlations [P5].

3. **Gene dosage effect:** The amount of functional polycystin protein ("dosage") affects the severity of disease expression [P4]. Lowering of PKD1 expression alone is sufficient to cause polycystic kidney disease [P5][P6], and functional polycystin-1 dosage governs disease severity [P5][P6]. Incompletely penetrant PKD1 alleles suggest a role for gene dosage in cyst initiation [P6].

4. **Multiple mutations can worsen disease:** Co-inheritance of mutations in multiple PKD genes (for example, inheriting both a PKD1 or PKD2 mutation along with mutations in other cystic kidney genes such as HNF1B or PKHD1) is associated with more severe phenotypes that can present very early in life, sometimes even prenatally, resembling autosomal recessive PKD [P4].

5. **Modifier genes also play a role:** Modifier genes play a significant role in the phenotypic expression of PKD1 disease. For instance, genetic variation in DKK3 may modify renal disease severity in PKD1 patients [P2].

In summary, while PKD1 and PKD2 are the major genes, it matters significantly which gene is affected and what type of mutation is present, as these factors strongly influence disease severity and the rate of progression toward kidney failure.

Occasionally, ADPKD can present prenatally or in infancy with a phenotype that resembles the classic presentation of ARPKD, a condition known as very early onset (VEO)-ADPKD. Some cases of VEO-ADPKD can be explained by coexistence of multiple PKD mutant alleles. Reported examples include coinheritance of an inactivating PKD1 allele from an affected parent and an incompletely penetrant or hypomorphic PKD1 allele from an unaffected parent [14-16], or coinheritance of two hypomorphic PKD1 alleles from unaffected or mildly affected parents [15, 17]. These findings suggest that the “dosage” of functional polycystin protein affects the severity of disease expression [15]. Co-inheritance of PKD1 or PKD2 mutations with other kidney cystic gene mutations such as HNF1B and PKHD1 is also associated with more severe ADPKD phenotypes that can resemble ARPKD [14]. question: Can PKD affect organs other than my kidneys? My doctor mentioned something about my liver too.
gold_answer:
Yes, PKD can definitely affect organs other than your kidneys, and your doctor is right to mention the liver. ADPKD is considered a systemic disease with numerous extrarenal manifestations [P4]. Here is what you should know:

**Liver cysts (Polycystic Liver Disease):**
The liver is the most commonly affected organ outside the kidneys. Polycystic liver disease (PLD) is characterized by the presence of multiple cysts scattered throughout the liver, which form due to overgrowth of the biliary epithelium [P4]. In both ADPKD and ARPKD, liver involvement is a major feature — in fact, liver cysts are so common in ADPKD that the disease is sometimes described as affecting mainly the kidney and liver [P2]. The severity of PLD can range widely, from just a few hepatic cysts to a massively enlarged cystic liver [P4]. There is typically a 0.9%–3.2% increase in liver size per year, and hepatomegaly can even occur before liver cysts are detected by imaging, possibly due to microscopic cysts, biliary epithelial proliferation, or hepatocyte hyperplasia [P3]. Importantly, cyst formation in the liver involves the same underlying mechanism as in the kidneys — a substantial reduction or complete loss of function of the polycystin proteins [P4]. Women with truncating PKD1 mutations may have a more severe liver phenotype compared to men [P6].

**Other affected organs:**
Beyond the liver, ADPKD is associated with cardiovascular abnormalities, including hypertension and left ventricular hypertrophy [P4]. Patients with ADPKD also have an increased predisposition to intracranial aneurysms [P2]. Additionally, ADPKD is associated with numerous extrarenal manifestations that arise in the gastrointestinal tract [P3].

**In ARPKD specifically:**
Liver involvement is also very significant. ARPKD is often accompanied by congenital hepatic fibrosis (CHF), and the disease is sometimes more appropriately called "ARPKD–congenital hepatic fibrosis" or "hepatorenal fibrocystic disease" to reflect the importance of liver involvement [P5]. Some ARPKD patients may present as adults with more prominent CHF [P2].

So in summary, PKD is not just a kidney disease — it is a systemic condition, and the liver is the most commonly involved extrarenal organ. Your doctor's mention of the liver is very appropriate, and monitoring of liver cysts is an important part of managing PKD.

Increased inflammation is probably an early event in disease progression, and the selective depletion of macrophages in the kidneys of a Pkd1 mouse model resulted in considerable amelioration of the cystic phenotype and improvement in renal function118. Macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF) is an important pro-inflammatory cytokine in PKD, as deletion of Mif delayed cyst growth and improved renal function in Pkd1 -knockout mice119. Extrarenal pathology.—ADPKD is a systemic disease and, in addition to the renal pathology, patients develop liver cysts and cardiovascular abnormalities. PLD is characterized by the presence of multiple cysts scattered throughout the liver parenchyma, which form owing to overgrowth of the biliary epithelium. The severity of PLD can range from a few hepatic cysts to a massively enlarged cystic liver. As in renal epithelia, a substantial reduction or complete loss of function of the polycystin proteins underlies liver cyst formation120. PLD can also occur as a distinct disease, without the involvement of renal cysts, as ADPLD. Although ADPLD is caused by mutations in a set of genes that are distinct from those involved in ADPKD, studies in mice suggest that mutations in these PLD genes affect the dosage of PC1, which seems to be the rate-limiting determinant of liver cyst formation121. question: What is the difference between autosomal dominant and autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease? Can both types run in the same family?
gold_answer:
Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) and autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease (ARPKD) are two distinct forms of PKD that differ in their genetic basis, inheritance pattern, age of onset, and clinical manifestations.

**Genetic basis:** ADPKD is caused by mutations in the PKD1 gene (located on chromosome 16p13.3, accounting for ~80% of cases) or the PKD2 gene (on chromosome 4q13, accounting for ~15% of cases) [P1][P2]. Patients with PKD1 mutations tend to have a more aggressive disease course compared to those with PKD2 mutations, though the two are clinically indistinguishable based solely on presentation [P1]. ARPKD is caused by mutations in the PKHD1 gene [P6].

**Inheritance pattern:** In ADPKD, only one copy of the mutated gene (inherited from one parent) is needed to cause disease — hence "dominant." In ARPKD, two copies of the mutated gene (one from each parent) must be inherited — hence "recessive." Despite these different inheritance patterns, both diseases belong to the family of genetic diseases known as "ciliopathies" and share common pathogenetic pathways that likely intersect at multiple levels [P1].

**Age of onset:** ADPKD is typically adult-onset [P2], whereas ARPKD generally presents much earlier, often in childhood or even the neonatal period [P2][P3]. ADPKD leads to end-stage renal disease (ESRD) in about 50% of patients [P2], and ARPKD is commonly associated with congenital hepatic fibrosis [P6].

**Clinical features:** Both diseases share the key manifestation of developing large, polycystic kidneys, but they are otherwise very different [P1]. They manifest in different regions of the renal tubule and show quite different extrarenal manifestations [P1]. For example, ADPKD is associated with hypertension (20–40% in children/adolescents, 50–70% in adults before GFR decline) and intracranial aneurysms (~8% of patients, with a three-fold increase if there is a positive family history) [P2].

**Can both types run in the same family?** Because ADPKD and ARPKD are caused by mutations in entirely different genes (PKD1/PKD2 versus PKHD1), they are genetically distinct diseases [P1][P6]. There is no described mechanism in the provided evidence by which both autosomal dominant and autosomal recessive forms would co-segregate within the same family as part of a single genetic trait. However, it is theoretically possible for unrelated mutations in different genes to coincidentally occur in the same family, though this would be exceedingly rare. The passages do not provide direct evidence or discussion of both ADPKD and ARPKD occurring together in a single family.

question: I've been reading about metabolic changes in polycystic kidneys — something about how the cells use energy differently. Can someone explain this in simple terms?
gold_answer:
Great question! In simple terms, the cells lining the cysts in polycystic kidneys use energy differently from normal kidney cells. Here's what happens:

**Normal kidney cells** generate most of their energy (ATP) through a process called oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS), which takes place in the mitochondria — the cell's 'power plants.' Glucose is broken down into pyruvate, which is then converted by the enzyme pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) into acetyl-CoA. This feeds into the TCA cycle (also called the Krebs cycle), which ultimately drives OXPHOS to produce a large amount of ATP efficiently. Fatty acid oxidation (FAO) also contributes to energy production in healthy cells [P3].

**In PKD cystic cells**, there is a major shift away from this efficient mitochondrial energy production. Instead, the cells rely much more heavily on glycolysis — a less efficient way of producing energy that converts glucose into lactate rather than feeding it into the mitochondria [P1]. This shift happens even when oxygen is available, which is a phenomenon known as the **Warburg effect** — something originally described in cancer cells [P4][P6].

Specifically, research showed that cells lacking the PKD1 gene had increased glucose uptake and converted more glucose into lactate. Key glycolytic enzymes like hexokinase 1 (HK1) and hexokinase 2 (HK2) were found to be upregulated in these cells [P2][P6]. When these cells were starved of glucose, they struggled to survive — showing decreased proliferation, increased cell death, and defective autophagy — which confirmed how dependent they had become on glucose for their energy [P6].

Why does this matter? Rapidly growing cells (like cyst-lining cells or cancer cells) use glycolysis not just for quick energy but also to efficiently convert glucose into the building blocks needed to construct new cellular components, fueling their abnormal growth [P6]. This metabolic reprogramming is thought to support the proliferation and fluid secretion that drive cyst expansion [P5].

Several important regulators are involved in this metabolic shift. The polycystin proteins (PC1 and PC2, encoded by the PKD1 and PKD2 genes) normally help regulate mitochondrial function — for example, the PC1–PC2 complex at mitochondria-associated membranes helps control oxidative phosphorylation by mediating calcium uptake into mitochondria [P5]. They also influence key energy-sensing pathways including AMPK, mTORC1, PPARα, PGC1α, and cAMP signaling [P1][P5]. When these proteins are dysfunctional due to mutations, mitochondrial energy production is impaired, and the cell compensates by shifting toward glycolysis.

Importantly, this metabolic vulnerability has been identified as a potential therapeutic target. In animal models, a glucose analogue called 2-deoxy-D-glucose (2-DG), which interferes with glucose utilization, was effective in inhibiting glycolysis and slowing disease progression [P2].

So in essence: PKD cells act a bit like cancer cells metabolically — they switch from efficient, oxygen-based energy production to a faster but less efficient glucose-dependent pathway, and this switch helps fuel the abnormal cell growth and fluid secretion that cause cysts to enlarge over time.

supporting_passages.text: Reviews Metabolism and mitochondria in polycystic kidney disease research and therapy Valeria Padovano1, Christine Podrini 2, Alessandra Boletta2 and Michael J. Caplan1* Abstract | Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is one of the most common, potentially lethal, monogenic diseases and is caused predominantly by mutations in polycystic kidney disease 1 (PKD1) and PKD2, which encode polycystin 1 (PC1) and PC2, respectively. Over the decades-l ong course of the disease, patients develop large fluid-f illed renal cysts that impair kidney function, leading to end-s tage renal disease in ~50% of patients. Despite the identification of numerous dysregulated pathways in ADPKD, the molecular mechanisms underlying the renal dysfunction from mutations in PKD genes and the physiological functions of the polycystin proteins are still unclear. Alterations in cell metabolism have emerged in the past decade as a hallmark of ADPKD. ADPKD cells shift their mode of energy production from oxidative phosphorylation to alternative pathways, such as glycolysis. In addition, the polycystins seem to play regulatory roles in modulating mechanisms and machinery related to energy production and utilization, including AMPK , PPARα, PGC1α, calcium signalling at mitochondria-a ssociated membranes, mTORC1, cAMP and CFTR-m ediated ion transport as well as the expression of crucial components of the mitochondrial energy production apparatus. In this Review , we explore these metabolic changes and discuss in detail

Furthermore, animal models of PKD and human-derived cystic epithelial cells showed increased expression of key glycolytic enzymes and this was mirrored, in the mouse kidney carrying Pkd1 deletion, by increased uptake of glucose and its conversion into lactate (214). Furthermore, and in line with this evidence, the administration of a glucose analogue, 2-deoxy-D-glucose (2-DG), previously demonstrated to interfere with glucose utilization in tissues, proved quite effective in inhibiting glycolysis in vivo and concomitantly improving disease progression in PKD murine models of increasing severity (214). These multiple lines of evidence prompted investigators to propose that ADPKD cystic epithelia were characterized by a Warburg effect similar to the one observed in cancer (213). question: What are the newest genetic discoveries about PKD? I know about PKD1 and PKD2, but are there other genes that have been linked to the disease recently?
gold_answer:
Yes, beyond PKD1 and PKD2, several additional genes have been linked to autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) in recent years. It is now estimated that approximately 5–10% of ADPKD cases are not caused by mutations in PKD1 or PKD2 [P1][P3]. Among the newly identified genes, GANAB is one of the most frequently cited minor ADPKD genes [P1][P3]. Additional genes that have been found to contribute to a smaller portion (less than 7%) of genetic diagnoses in ADPKD include IFT140, DNAJB11, NEK8, ALG5, ALG8, and ALG9 [P3]. The 2025 KDIGO guidelines have recommended a standardized nomenclature where the disease name is followed by the causative gene (e.g., ADPKD-PKD1 or ADPKD-PKD2) to improve clarity in classification [P3].

It is important to note that cases caused by these minor genes tend to present atypically compared to classic ADPKD — for example, patients may develop multiple very large cysts that are limited in number and have a different echogenicity on imaging compared to typical ADPKD kidneys [P1]. This atypical presentation is why, for decades, nearly all clinically diagnosed ADPKD cases were attributed to PKD1 or PKD2, with only rare exceptions [P1].

Additionally, compound heterozygosity — where a PKD1 variant coexists with a mutation in another cystic gene such as PKD2, COL4A1, or HNF1B — has been linked to worsened disease severity [P3]. Some clinically detected ADPKD cases that test negative for PKD1 and PKD2 may also be due to deep intronic variations affecting gene splicing or genes not yet discovered [P3].

For autosomal recessive PKD (ARPKD), the primary gene is PKHD1, which encodes fibrocystin [P1]. More recently, mutations in DZIP1L, which encodes a ciliary/basal body protein, have been described in some ARPKD families who lack PKHD1 mutations, though DZIP1L mutations appear to cause a relatively moderate ARPKD phenotype [P4].

In summary, the genetic landscape of PKD has expanded considerably beyond PKD1 and PKD2, with genes like GANAB, IFT140, DNAJB11, NEK8, ALG5, ALG8, ALG9, and DZIP1L now recognized as contributors to different forms and severities of polycystic kidney disease.

Minor PKD genes (e.g. GANAB): Finally, as suggested above, more recent clinical and genetic investigations have described additional minor genes involved in Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease. Indeed, today it is estimated that about 5% of ADPKD cases are not caused by mutations in either PKD1 or PKD2 (10). For clarity, it should be stressed here that all these cases are described to be atypical cases of ADPKD, with an unusual clinical presentation that can include the formation of multiple very large cysts that are limited in number, with an echogenicity that is different from that of typical ADPKD kidneys. This explains why for two decades almost the totality of patients that were diagnosed with ADPKD based on clinical criteria had been ultimately associated with mutations in either the PKD1 or PKD2 genes, with only very few exceptions.
