supporting_passages.text: Discussion We anticipate that there will continue to be a need for molecular diagnosis among individuals from ADPKD families. DNA testing may be relevant in a variety of clinical circumstances including the evaluation of living donors from ADPKD families, early detection for treatment with new therapeutic agents and in individuals with atypical clinical presentations where a diagnosis of ADPKD is in question. In situations where ultrasound is not sufficiently sensitive and where family members are unavailable for linkage studies, direct DNA testing has the potential to provide genetic information to an isolated proband. Direct sequencing of PKD1 and PKD2 forms of the basis for the analysis in this study. We have evaluated the utility of this test in 82 individuals with a clinical diagnosis of ADPKD. We found that 42% of the study participants (N=34) had a definitive pathogenic mutation predicted to result in premature truncation of either PKD protein (Class I). The remaining study subjects had in frame deletions/insertions, amino acid substitutions or intronic changes requiring further analysis to assess pathogenicity (Class II). We chose not to study the segregation of these variants since the lack of available family members is an important indication for the use of direct sequencing. Ultrasound imaging might not provide a sufficiently certain diagnosis in at risk individuals younger than 30 years of age who wish to donate a kidney to a relative with renal failure. In addition, clinicians may encounter patients with atypical cystic disease in whom the diagnosis may not be obvious. Finally, new therapies for ADPKD are on the horizon and there is evidence to suggest that these may be most effective if initiated early in the disease course, perhaps even before cystic disease is apparent [10–12]. Linkage analysis for ADPKD has been commercially available for some time, but requires the participation of at least two and preferably more affected family members. In cases where family members are not available, direct DNA analysis offers the possibility of establishing a molecular diagnosis. Direct DNA testing for ADPKD has posed a unique set of challenges. PKD1 analysis in particular has been complicated because the 5′ portion of the gene (exons 1– 34) is replicated in at least 5 highly homologous copies (<2% divergence) elsewhere on chromosome 16 [6]. question: What’s the best test to diagnose ADPKD—ultrasound, CT, or MRI—and when would one be preferred over the others?
gold_answer:
Bottom line: Use kidney ultrasound first to diagnose ADPKD in people at risk (those with a positive family history); it is recommended as the initial test because it is noninvasive, generally available, and inexpensive [P4][P5].

When to prefer MRI over ultrasound:
- If ultrasound is equivocal or greater resolution is needed to detect very small cysts; MRI offers superior sensitivity for very small cysts and can detect cysts ≥2 mm [P4][P1].
- In young at-risk adults (especially <30 years) where ultrasound sensitivity is lower; if ultrasound is equivocal, MRI is advised [P1].
- When ultrasound image quality is limited by large body habitus [P1].
- In settings where MRI cost and availability are similar to ultrasound; MRI is an excellent alternative for screening at-risk people [P1].
- Unenhanced MRI has better resolution than unenhanced CT for small cysts [P3].

When to prefer CT:
- If concurrent pathologies are suspected (e.g., stone disease, solid kidney mass, kidney hemorrhage, hydronephrosis), CT can be particularly useful [P1].
- With intravenous contrast, CT is highly sensitive for kidney cysts down to 2 mm, though this must be balanced against contrast and radiation considerations [P1][P3].

Safety, contraindications, and practical considerations that guide choice:
- CT employs ionizing radiation, and the use of intravenous contrast can be a risk in people with abnormal kidney function [P3].
- MRI may be contraindicated with certain implanted devices or retained metal, and some patients have discomfort in the magnet [P3].
- If initial ultrasound results are equivocal or atypical, follow-up imaging with MRI or CT can help secure the diagnosis; genetic testing can also clarify the diagnosis when imaging is uncertain [P6][P1][P3].
- Ultrasound is the least costly and most widely available modality; MRI and CT availability/cost vary, so selection should consider local resources and the resolution needed, in consultation with radiology [P5].

question: Should I have my children tested for ADPKD now or wait until they’re older, and what kinds of screening are recommended?
gold_answer:
Short answer: Both paths are acceptable. After counselling, families may reasonably choose either immediate diagnostic testing (ultrasound and/or genetic testing) or to defer diagnosis while doing regular clinical screening for treatable features; the decision should be shared with clinicians and involve minors when possible [P1][P5][P3]. Reasons some families defer include: no clear evidence that presymptomatic detection improves outcomes in children, limited proven treatments for slowing progression in childhood, and the ethical consideration of preserving a child’s autonomy to decide later; insurance and career implications of establishing a diagnosis also need consideration and vary by country [P1][P6].

If you want a diagnosis now
- Imaging: Renal ultrasonography is the current radiological method of choice for screening at‑risk children; finding one or more cysts is highly suggestive of ADPKD, but a negative scan cannot rule out ADPKD in childhood [P2]. Because cysts appear gradually, sensitivity is better in older than in younger children and with high‑resolution ultrasonography; the negative predictive value of a normal childhood scan is limited [P3].
- Genetic testing: Recommended for infants with very‑early‑onset symptomatic disease and for children with a negative family history who have progressive disease; otherwise, it is not routinely indicated but can be considered after counselling as one of the immediate diagnostic options [P2][P5]. Note that imaging criteria validated in young adults (for example, ≥3 renal cysts on ultrasonography or ≥10 on MRI) were not derived from populations under 15 years, which limits certainty in younger children [P5].

If you prefer to wait on diagnostic confirmation
- Regular clinical screening without diagnostic testing is an equally valid approach for asymptomatic at‑risk minors; focus on detecting treatable but often silent manifestations (hypertension and proteinuria) [P5]. Children with a positive family history, whether their status is confirmed or unknown, should be monitored for hypertension (preferably with ambulatory blood pressure monitoring) and albuminuria; measurement of blood pressure and proteinuria should be performed at the same intervals as for children with proven disease [P2][P1]. Practical feasibility of community blood‑pressure monitoring may vary and should be considered [P1]. If diagnostic screening is deferred in childhood, parents should be aware of their responsibility to inform their children about disease risk when they reach the age of majority [P3].

What is not recommended as routine screening in children with ADPKD
- Intracranial aneurysm screening is not recommended [P4].
- Regular screening for liver cysts is not recommended [P4].

When to seek specialist referral
- Very‑early‑onset ADPKD or ARPKD‑like presentations warrant early referral to a specialized centre [P4].

supporting_passages.text: the current radiological method of choice for screening. Sonographic detection of one or more cysts in an at-r isk child is highly suggestive of ADPKD, but a negative scan cannot rule out ADPKD in childhood. Genetic testing is recommended for infants with very-e arly-onset symptomatic disease and for children with a negative family history and progressive disease. Children with a positive family history and either confirmed or unknown disease status should be monitored for hypertension (preferably by ambulatory blood pressure monitoring) and albuminuria. Currently, vasopressin antagonists should not be offered routinely but off-l abel use can be considered in selected children. No consensus was reached on the use of statins, but mTOR inhibitors and somatostatin analogues are not recommended. Children with ADPKD should be strongly encouraged to achieve the low dietary salt intake that is recommended for all children. question: Should I be screened for brain aneurysms because I have ADPKD, and who actually needs that test?
gold_answer:
Short answer: Not everyone with ADPKD needs screening for brain aneurysms; presymptomatic screening for all patients is not generally recommended, and universal consensus has not been reached [P5]. Screening is clearly recommended for people with ADPKD who have a personal history of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) or a positive family history of intracranial aneurysm (ICA), SAH, or unexplained sudden death, provided they would be eligible for treatment and have a reasonable life expectancy [P4]. There is strong consensus to recommend screening when the positive family history is in a first-degree relative [P2].

Who should be screened or have screening discussed:
- Definite: ADPKD plus personal SAH, or positive family history of ICA/SAH/unexplained sudden death, in those eligible for treatment and with reasonable life expectancy [P4], with especially strong consensus when the history is in a first-degree relative [P2].
- Consider/discuss: de novo ADPKD, unknown or limited family history, or personal/familial extracerebral vascular phenotypes [P4].
- Consider in specific settings: during evaluation for kidney and/or liver transplantation or before major elective surgery [P4], and when required by occupational health services for high‑risk jobs (for example, bus drivers), with expert opinion also citing high‑risk professions such as pilots [P2][P5].

How much higher is the yield with family history: Among people with ADPKD and a positive family history of SAH or ICA who undergo screening, the frequency of detecting an ICA is about 17%, roughly four times higher than in ADPKD overall, and is maximal when a first-degree relative had SAH or when ≥2 relatives have ICA/SAH [P4]. Notably, 50–60% of ADPKD patients diagnosed with ICA and about 40% of those with ruptured ICA report no family history, so some people without clear family history may still elect screening after counseling and should be given access [P4][P6].

Which test to use: Time‑of‑flight magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) without gadolinium is the recommended imaging modality when screening is pursued; high‑resolution computed tomography angiography (CTA) can be used as an alternative [P1]. MRA typically uses the time‑of‑flight method without gadolinium and is a safe diagnostic method in the absence of contraindications, though some people experience claustrophobia [P2]. CTA can be considered initially, but iodinated contrast may worsen kidney function—especially if eGFR <30 ml/min/1.73 m²—and can rarely cause allergic reactions; radiation exposure is also a concern, and artifacts from bone or prior coils/stents may limit usefulness [P2]. Both MRA and CTA detect aneurysms ≥5 mm reliably, while smaller aneurysms (down to 2 mm) are detected less reliably [P1].

Why not screen everyone: Most aneurysms detected by presymptomatic screening are small (<7 mm) and in the anterior circulation, which in the general population have a very small risk of rupture, and limited data suggest the natural history of unruptured aneurysms in ADPKD resembles that of the general population; accordingly, expert reviews do not recommend screening all ADPKD patients [P5]. A single‑center study suggested systematic screening could be cost‑effective, but its aneurysm rupture rate was five times higher than other ADPKD cohorts, limiting generalizability [P2].

Shared decision‑making: People should be counseled before imaging about benefits and harms, including potential anxiety and implications of an intracranial finding (for example, future life‑insurance issues) [P1][P6]. Preventive aneurysm occlusion carries an approximately 5–8% complication risk, which reduces the net benefit of screening and should be factored into decisions [P6].

question: In ADPKD, what kidney measurement do scans often track to see how big the kidneys are getting over time?
gold_answer: Total kidney volume (TKV) is the key measurement tracked on imaging to monitor kidney enlargement over time; it is often expressed as height‑adjusted TKV (htTKV) and can be measured by MRI, CT, or ultrasound [P1][P3].

Other clinical factors: Other clinical factors that have been associated with kidney disease progression in adolescents and adults with ADPKD include male sex, higher body mass index, higher serum uric acid, higher sodium intake, lower serum HDL cholesterol [29, 45, 46]. Although there are no randomized clinical trials to show that lifestyle interventions can slow progression in children with ADPKD, expert recommendations include healthy lifestyle measures that are beneficial in the general population and individuals with CKD [47]. These include maintaining normal weight, limiting dietary salt intake, ensuring adequate water intake to prevent dehydration, and avoiding excessive protein intake [47]. Imaging predictors—Total kidney volume (TKV) and cyst volume increase progressively in adults and children with ADPKD, and increases in TKV generally precede a decline in GFR [33, 48, 49]. Long-term follow up of adult participants in the Consortium for Radiologic Imaging Studies of PKD (CRISP) has shown that baseline height-adjusted total kidney volume (htTKV) measured by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is strongly predictive of GFR decline over a 13-year period [50]. question: What is the name of the imaging risk tool that uses age and height‑adjusted total kidney volume to help predict how fast ADPKD might progress?
gold_answer: MIC (referred to as “MiC” in the passages)—an htTKV-based imaging classification used to stratify ADPKD progression risk; TKV interpretation should be considered in relation to patient age [P4][P1][P6].

MRI is the most accurate method, and is generally preferred in research contexts due to the lack of precision of US in detecting short-term disease progression [51, 52]. In children with early ADPKD, US can provide a reasonable estimate of TKV since the kidneys can usually fit within the US probe field of view [34]. However, the degree of measurement error by US increases with larger TKV [53]. question: I just got diagnosed with ADPKD. Are there any medications available right now that can slow down the cyst growth?
gold_answer:
Yes, there is currently one FDA-approved medication specifically for ADPKD: tolvaptan, a vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist (V2RA). It is the only drug that has been proven to attenuate disease progression [P4]. Tolvaptan works by slowing cyst growth and has been shown to delay the onset of renal failure by an average of approximately 5 years after 18 years of treatment [P1]. It remains effective even at eGFR levels as low as 15 mL/min and is recommended until kidney replacement therapy is required [P6]. However, tolvaptan can be difficult to tolerate due to aquaresis-related side effects such as nausea and polyuria, and it has been found to cause hepatotoxicity in certain patients, which limits its widespread use [P1][P4].

Beyond tolvaptan, several other drugs are being actively investigated in clinical trials but are not yet approved for ADPKD:

- **Metformin**: Already marketed for type 2 diabetes, metformin has a well-established safety profile and may slow cyst progression through activation of AMPK and effects on glucose metabolism. The TAME (Metformin as a Novel Therapy for ADPKD) trial and other clinical trials are currently evaluating its use in ADPKD patients [P3][P2].

- **Somatostatin analogues** (e.g., octreotide, lanreotide): These decrease cAMP levels and have shown favorable trends in reducing total kidney volume (TKV) increase, though statistical significance was not reached in the ALADIN study, and larger trials such as DIPAK1 and LIPS are ongoing [P5].

- **Pravastatin**: In a randomized trial in children and young adults with ADPKD (ages 8–22), pravastatin was found to decrease the rate of TKV growth [P2]. However, a post-hoc analysis of adult participants in the HALT PKD study found no benefit, and a randomized trial in adults is underway [P2].

- **Other agents under investigation** include curcumin (in children and young adults), bardoxolone, tesevatinib (a multikinase EGF/Src inhibitor), CFTR modulators, and micro RNA inhibitors [P2][P4].

- **mTOR inhibitors** (sirolimus, everolimus) were initially promising in animal models but failed to show benefit in clinical trials and were associated with unacceptable side effects [P2][P5].

It is important to note that while tolvaptan and other investigational drugs may slow disease progression, none of them cure the underlying genetic defect of PKD, and no therapies currently exist that can reverse cyst expansion [P1]. Emerging approaches such as anti-miRNA therapies, CRISPR technologies, and other gene therapy solutions may hold future promise [P1]. You should discuss with your nephrologist whether tolvaptan is appropriate for you based on your risk of disease progression and ability to tolerate its side effects.

supporting_passages.paper_title: Targeting and Therapeutic Peptide-Based Strategies for Polycystic Kidney Disease
supporting_passages.text: management and protein restricted diet ultimately show minimal benefits in delaying the age of ESRD onset due to cyst progression [8]. Recent efforts have focused on the use of drugs already approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for other diseases that have also shown efficacy in delaying cystogenesis, including pioglitazone, sirolimus, and metformin (Table 1) [9–12]. Despite initial enthusiasm, these drugs have been largely disappointing due to poor tolerability, offtarget organ side effects, and/or limited therapeutic efficacy in patients [13]. Similarly, tolvaptan, the only FDA approved drug for autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), is difficult to tolerate due to side effects such as nausea and polyuria, and has been found to cause hepatotoxicity in certain patients [14]. Although tolvaptan slows cyst growth and delays the onset of renal failure by an average of ~5 years after 18 years of treatment, like other small molecules investigated for PKD, tolvaptan does not cure the underlying genetic defect of PKD [15]. Currently, no clinical trials exist for therapies that reverse cyst expansion, but emerging anti-micro RNA (anti-miR) [16, 17], clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) technologies [18], or other gene therapy solutions may provide promise for PKD reversal upon further investigation. question: Are there any new drugs being tested right now for ADPKD besides the one that's already approved? What are they targeting?
gold_answer:
Yes, beyond tolvaptan — the only FDA-approved disease-modifying drug for ADPKD [P1][P3] — a number of new and repurposed drugs are being investigated in clinical trials and preclinical studies, targeting a variety of pathways:

1. **Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator (CFTR) Modulators and MicroRNA Inhibitors:** These are among the new drug classes currently being investigated in clinical trials for ADPKD. CFTR modulators target chloride-driven fluid secretion into cysts, while microRNA inhibitors (anti-miR strategies) aim to correct dysregulated gene expression involved in cyst growth [P5][P4].

2. **Metformin:** Already marketed for diabetes, metformin is being investigated for off-label use in ADPKD [P5]. It has shown some efficacy in delaying cystogenesis in preclinical models, though clinical results have been mixed [P4].

3. **Somatostatin Analogues:** These drugs, which target hormonal signaling pathways that influence cAMP levels and fluid secretion, have been studied in ADPKD, though data on their renoprotective effects differ substantially between studies and specific medications [P5].

4. **STAT Pathway Inhibitors (Drug Repurposing Candidates):** Several FDA-approved drugs targeting the STAT signaling family are being explored. These include pimozide (which inhibits STAT5), crizotinib (which inhibits STAT3 phosphorylation), and pyrimethamine (which inhibits STAT3 dimerization). Pyrimethamine has been shown to decrease cell proliferation in ADPKD patient-derived cells and prevent renal cyst formation in mouse models [P2].

5. **Other Repurposed Drugs:** Additional repurposing candidates include fenofibrate, which reduced renal cyst volume, proliferation, and inflammatory cell infiltration in a mouse model of PKD [P2]; paclitaxel, a cell-cycle arrest agent studied for its potential to inhibit cyst growth [P2]; nilotinib, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor also named as a top repurposing candidate [P2]; and pioglitazone and sirolimus, which target metabolic and mTOR pathways, respectively, though these have been largely disappointing in clinical translation due to poor tolerability and limited efficacy [P4].

6. **Computational Drug Repurposing via Signature Reversion:** A systematic approach using gene expression data from PKD mouse models and the LINCS drug response database has been used to identify additional FDA-approved drug candidates whose molecular signatures inversely match the disease gene expression profile, aiming to accelerate identification of new ADPKD therapeutics that target cAMP signaling, inflammation, and metabolic reprogramming pathways [P6].

7. **Emerging Gene-Based Therapies:** CRISPR gene editing and anti-miR technologies are in early-stage investigation and aim to address the underlying genetic defect in PKD, though no clinical trials currently exist for therapies that reverse cyst expansion [P4].

In summary, while tolvaptan remains the sole approved therapy [P3], active investigation spans multiple drug classes and molecular targets — including cAMP and fluid secretion pathways, STAT signaling, metabolic pathways (mTOR, AMPK), cell-cycle regulators, inflammatory pathways, and gene therapy approaches [P2][P4][P5][P6].

Despite initial enthusiasm, these drugs have been largely disappointing due to poor tolerability, offtarget organ side effects, and/or limited therapeutic efficacy in patients [13]. Similarly, tolvaptan, the only FDA approved drug for autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), is difficult to tolerate due to side effects such as nausea and polyuria, and has been found to cause hepatotoxicity in certain patients [14]. Although tolvaptan slows cyst growth and delays the onset of renal failure by an average of ~5 years after 18 years of treatment, like other small molecules investigated for PKD, tolvaptan does not cure the underlying genetic defect of PKD [15]. Lasseigne1* Abstract Background Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is one of the most prevalent monogenic human diseases. It is mostly caused by pathogenic variants in PKD1 or PKD2 genes that encode interacting transmembrane proteins polycystin-1 (PC1) and polycystin-2 (PC2). Among many pathogenic processes described in ADPKD, those associated with cAMP signaling, inflammation, and metabolic reprogramming appear to regulate the disease manifestations. Tolvaptan, a vasopressin receptor-2 antagonist that regulates cAMP pathway, is the only FDAapproved ADPKD therapeutic. Tolvaptan reduces renal cyst growth and kidney function loss, but it is not tolerated by many patients and is associated with idiosyncratic liver toxicity. Therefore, additional therapeutic options for ADPKD treatment are needed. question: How does the medication tolvaptan actually work in the body to slow down cyst growth? And what are the main side effects I should expect?
gold_answer:
Tolvaptan is a vasopressin V2 receptor (V2R) antagonist — it works by blocking the V2R on the principal cells of the renal collecting duct [P6]. Under normal circumstances, the antidiuretic hormone vasopressin binds to the V2R and stimulates the production of cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) inside these cells [P6]. In ADPKD, the cAMP signaling pathway is abnormally upregulated in cystic epithelial cells, which drives increased cell proliferation and fluid secretion into the cysts, causing them to enlarge [P4]. By blocking the V2R, tolvaptan reduces vasopressin-induced cAMP accumulation in V2R-expressing cells [P1][P6], thereby slowing the rate of cyst growth (measured as total kidney volume increase) and helping to preserve kidney function [P2][P6].

It is important to note that tolvaptan's molecular target — the V2R — is expressed primarily in collecting duct principal cells, so other cAMP agonists present in ADPKD cyst fluid can still stimulate cAMP production through V2R-independent pathways, which may limit tolvaptan's overall efficacy [P1][P6].

**Main side effects to be aware of:**

1. **Aquaresis / Polyuria:** Because tolvaptan blocks the receptor that normally helps concentrate urine, it causes the excretion of large volumes of dilute urine (aquaresis). This is one of the most common side effects and can significantly affect quality of life, sometimes leading patients to discontinue therapy [P1][P2][P4].

2. **Hepatotoxicity / Liver dysfunction:** Tolvaptan carries a risk of potentially life-threatening liver injury. Deranged liver function tests have been detected in clinical trials, making regular liver monitoring essential [P1][P2][P4].

3. **Other reported side effects** include dry mouth, hypernatremia (elevated blood sodium), nausea and vomiting, and abdominal pain or cramps [P2].

Because of these side effects — particularly the aquaretic burden and the risk of liver toxicity — and the need to potentially take the medication for decades, tolvaptan is recommended only for adult ADPKD patients who are at risk for rapid disease progression [P1][P4].

supporting_passages.text: Although this earlier study had several limitations including small sample size and lack of randomization, it implies potential efficacy of cinacalcet in ADPKD patients. Our findings here suggest that cinacalcet can potentially be efficacious at earlier stages of ADPKD to slow down disease progression. Tolvaptan is the only FDA-approved ADPKD treatment; however, its use is restricted to adult patients at risk for rapid progression, since its efficacy was less prominent in other patient groups.34 Tolvaptan blocks V2R which results in reduced vasopressin-induced cAMP elevation in cyst and tubule epithelia. Although vasopressin is considered as the key physiological cAMP agonist in distal nephron, earlier studies showed that ADPKD cyst fluid contains other cAMP agonists2, 8, 9 which can potentially stimulate V2 receptor-independent Cl− and fluid secretion, and limit efficacy of tolvaptan. Other than hepatotoxicity, polyuria is another common side effect of tolvaptan affecting patients’ quality of life and sometimes leading to treatment discontinuation.35 Since polyuria is not a known side effect of cinacalcet, it can potentially be used in ADPKD without any effects on urine output. Since cinacalcet and tolvaptan work through different receptors, they can potentially be used in combination for higher efficacy. This idea is also supported by a recent study that showed synergistic effects of CaSR activator R-568 in mouse and rat models of ADPKD when used together

supporting_passages.text: have found that treatment with V2 receptor antagonists can reduce cystic burden in orthologous animal models of human PKD by reducing renal cAMP levels [87, 168]. These studies eventually motivated development of the V2 receptor antagonist, tolvaptan, which received FDA approval in 2018 [169]. In the first seminal clinical trial, the Tolvaptan Efficacy and Safety in Management of Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease and Its Outcomes (TEMPO) 3:4, tolvaptan, as compared with placebo, slowed the increase in total kidney volume and the decline in kidney function. Despite these outcomes, this first generation, small molecule drug also has undesirable drawbacks. Patients treated with tolvaptan exhibit adverse events related to aquaresis (excretion of dilute urine) and liver toxicity, which led to a higher discontinuation rate (23% dropout rate vs. 14% in the placebo group). Deranged liver function tests detected in clinical trials were found to be potentially life-threatening, and additional, major side effects include dry mouth, polyuria, hypernatremia, nausea/vomiting, and abdominal pain/cramps [170]. Notably, expected therapeutic outcomes have also been lackluster, as perfect adherence to tolvaptan treatment for 18 years is predicted to delay the time to ESRD by only 4.9 years [15]. The oral bioavailability of tolvaptan is expected to be 42% [171], and these drawbacks of off-target side effects and limited therapeutic efficacy calls for opportunities in precision medicine using

Increased liver cysts are also seen in females with multiple pregnancies, again demonstrating the likely effect of estrogen on liver cyst development [6, 72]. Liver cyst volume and TKV have been found to be positively correlated in some studies [6, 71], but this correlation disappeared when adjusted for age, suggesting that it simply represents simultaneous progression of liver and kidney disease with age. In contrast to kidney involvement, genotype does not appear to be a significant predictor of the severity of polycystic liver disease in patients with ADPKD [73]. Pharmacologic agents to prevent ADPKD progression Pathophysiologic mechanisms of cyst enlargement in ADPKD involve a number of biological pathways. The cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) pathway is upregulated in PKD cystic epithelia [74], leading to increased cell proliferation and fluid secretion [10]. cAMP activity can be downregulated by antagonism of arginine vasopressin (AVP) activity. The vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist tolvaptan has been shown to slow TKV growth and GFR decline, and is now FDA-approved for patients with ADPKD over 18 years of age [57]. Given the potential for side effects such as aquaresis and liver dysfunction [57], and the burden of potentially taking a medication for decades, tolvaptan is only recommended for adults at risk for rapid ADPKD progression [66]. Signaling pathways Perturbations of the cAMP signaling pathway were the first ADPKD-related cellular abnormalities to be explored in depth and, fittingly, are the first to be addressed by an approved therapy. Stimulation of the V2R vasopressin receptor by anti-diuretic hormone induces cAMP production in the principal cells of the renal collecting duct (429). Administration of Tolvaptan, a small molecule inhibitor of the V2R, reduces cAMP accumulation in V2R-expressing cells and slows cyst development in numerous animal models of ADPKD (430). More importantly, administration of this compound slows cyst growth and preserves renal function in ADPKD patients (431). Consequently, Tolvaptan has obtained international regulatory approval as a therapeutic for ADPKD. It is worth noting that other approaches to reducing renal epithelial cell cAMP production, including among others by activating the somatostatin receptor or by inhibiting the β3 adrenergic receptor, have also been explored (432) and may be indicative of alternatives that have the potential Physiol Rev. Small molecules that target additional perturbed signaling pathways have also shown substantial promise

question: I've been reading about how tolvaptan affects your kidneys in the short term — like, does it start working right away to change kidney size, or does it take a while to see any effect?
gold_answer:
Great question! Tolvaptan actually does start working quite quickly on the kidneys. In a clinical study of 27 ADPKD patients, researchers examined the short-term effects of tolvaptan after just 3 weeks of treatment (with increasing doses of 60, 90, and 120 mg/day in weeks 1, 2, and 3, respectively) [P1]. Within that short 3-week period, treatment led to measurable changes: there was a decrease in total kidney volume (TKV), along with an increase in urine volume and free-water clearance, and a decrease in urine osmolality and kidney injury marker excretion [P1]. So yes, tolvaptan begins to have detectable effects on kidney size and other kidney-related markers within just a few weeks of starting treatment.

However, there are some important nuances. The magnitude of these short-term effects depended on the patient's baseline kidney function. Patients with lower baseline kidney function (lower measured GFR) showed smaller changes in urine volume, urine osmolality, and TKV compared to those with better-preserved kidney function [P1]. Interestingly though, the change in fractional free-water clearance was actually greater in patients with lower GFRs, suggesting that tolvaptan was having a stronger effect per functioning nephron in those patients, which the researchers interpreted as a sign that patients with reduced kidney function might still benefit from long-term treatment [P1].

It's worth noting that while these short-term effects on kidney size are encouraging, the major long-term clinical benefit of tolvaptan is a reduction in the rate of kidney function decline of about 25%–33%, which translates to a potential delay in needing kidney replacement therapy by about 1 year for every 3–4 years of treatment [P2]. The landmark TEMPO 3:4 trial also confirmed that tolvaptan, compared with placebo, slowed both the increase in total kidney volume and the decline in kidney function over longer periods [P5].

So in summary: tolvaptan does begin to change kidney size and other markers within weeks, but the full clinical benefits in terms of slowing disease progression are realized over longer-term treatment.

These studies eventually motivated development of the V2 receptor antagonist, tolvaptan, which received FDA approval in 2018 [169]. In the first seminal clinical trial, the Tolvaptan Efficacy and Safety in Management of Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease and Its Outcomes (TEMPO) 3:4, tolvaptan, as compared with placebo, slowed the increase in total kidney volume and the decline in kidney function. Despite these outcomes, this first generation, small molecule drug also has undesirable drawbacks. Patients treated with tolvaptan exhibit adverse events related to aquaresis (excretion of dilute urine) and liver toxicity, which led to a higher discontinuation rate (23% dropout rate vs. 14% in the placebo group). Deranged liver function tests detected in clinical trials were found to be potentially life-threatening, and additional, major side effects include dry mouth, polyuria, hypernatremia, nausea/vomiting, and abdominal pain/cramps [170]. Notably, expected therapeutic outcomes have also been lackluster, as perfect adherence to tolvaptan treatment for 18 years is predicted to delay the time to ESRD by only 4.9 years [15]. question: Are there any new drugs being developed for ADPKD besides tolvaptan? I've heard there might be other options in the pipeline.
gold_answer:
Yes, there is significant interest in developing new therapies for ADPKD beyond tolvaptan, which remains the only FDA-approved disease-modifying treatment for the condition [P1][P2]. While tolvaptan effectively slows disease progression, it causes serious side effects with long-term use, including idiosyncratic liver toxicity, and is not tolerated by many patients, creating a clear need for additional therapeutic options [P1][P4].

Several promising avenues are being explored:

1. **Somatostatin analogues**: These target somatostatin receptors (SSTR) and work to reduce cyst growth through mechanisms related to cAMP reduction, similar in concept to tolvaptan but via a different receptor pathway [P6].

2. **mTOR inhibitors**: The mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway, which regulates cell growth and proliferation, is being investigated as a therapeutic target in ADPKD [P6].

3. **EGFR (Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor) inhibitors**: These are considered significant for therapeutic intervention in ADPKD, as EGFR signaling is implicated in cyst development [P6].

4. **Metabolic-targeting approaches**: Research has shown that ADPKD cells have metabolic alterations, including dependence on glycolysis and glutamine. Interventions such as 2-deoxyglucose (which starves glycolysis-dependent cyst cells) and ketosis-inducing diets have shown promise in animal models of ADPKD [P3].

5. **Drug repurposing candidates**: Computational approaches, such as signature reversion using the Library of Integrated Network-Based Cellular Signatures (LINCS), are being used to identify existing FDA-approved drugs that might be repurposed for ADPKD, which could significantly decrease the time and cost compared to traditional drug discovery [P4].

It is important to note, however, that various treatment options have shown discrepant results between preclinical (animal) and clinical (human) studies, so not all promising candidates will necessarily succeed in clinical trials [P5]. The pathway pioneered by the investigators who brought tolvaptan from the laboratory to the clinic has established a framework for testing and approving future therapies that may offer better efficacy and side effect profiles [P3]. Many research teams continue to work on identifying new molecular mechanisms and potential targets in PKD [P1], and there remains a large need for additional effective treatments to provide more personalized care for ADPKD patients [P2].

Lasseigne1* Abstract Background Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is one of the most prevalent monogenic human diseases. It is mostly caused by pathogenic variants in PKD1 or PKD2 genes that encode interacting transmembrane proteins polycystin-1 (PC1) and polycystin-2 (PC2). Among many pathogenic processes described in ADPKD, those associated with cAMP signaling, inflammation, and metabolic reprogramming appear to regulate the disease manifestations. Tolvaptan, a vasopressin receptor-2 antagonist that regulates cAMP pathway, is the only FDAapproved ADPKD therapeutic. Tolvaptan reduces renal cyst growth and kidney function loss, but it is not tolerated by many patients and is associated with idiosyncratic liver toxicity. Therefore, additional therapeutic options for ADPKD treatment are needed. question: I know tolvaptan is the main drug for ADPKD, but what are the side effects? My doctor mentioned something about liver problems and having to pee a lot.
gold_answer:
Your doctor is right to highlight those two key areas. Here is a comprehensive overview of tolvaptan's side effects:

**1. Aquaretic (water-loss) side effects — the frequent urination your doctor mentioned:**
Because tolvaptan blocks the vasopressin V2 receptor in the kidney, it causes the body to excrete large volumes of dilute urine. The anticipated aquaretic adverse events include polyuria (excessive urination), nocturia (waking at night to urinate), pollakiuria (frequent urination during the day), and excessive thirst [P1][P2][P4]. You need to drink enough water to replace what you lose in urine [P1][P2]. These effects are directly related to the drug's mechanism of action and are essentially expected rather than unexpected [P4].

**2. Liver problems (hepatotoxicity):**
Tolvaptan can cause derangement of liver function tests (LFTs), which has been identified as potentially life-threatening [P6]. This risk is highest within the first 18 months of treatment, during which monthly liver and kidney function monitoring is mandatory [P4][P1]. Baseline liver function (AST, ALT, total bilirubin) must be measured before starting the drug, and frequent monitoring must continue throughout treatment [P1]. If liver function abnormalities are detected, tolvaptan must be interrupted immediately [P1].

**3. Other reported side effects:**
Additional side effects reported in clinical trials include dry mouth, hypernatremia (high blood sodium), nausea and vomiting, and abdominal pain or cramps [P6].

**4. Important safety precautions:**
Because of the aquaretic effect, tolvaptan must be immediately stopped in situations that cause volume depletion or when you cannot drink enough water to compensate for urinary losses — for example, during vomiting, diarrhea, fever, excessive sweating, or when access to water is limited (such as hiking, traveling, or long flights) [P1][P2][P4]. A "sick-day plan" should be established with your doctor so you know exactly when to skip doses to avoid dehydration and acute kidney injury [P1][P4]. Treatment should also be paused if proper liver function monitoring cannot be carried out [P4].

**5. Discontinuation rates:**
In the landmark TEMPO 3:4 clinical trial, aquaretic side effects and liver toxicity contributed to a higher dropout rate in the tolvaptan group (23%) compared with placebo (14%) [P6].

In summary, the two concerns your doctor raised — liver problems and frequent urination — are indeed the most important side effects of tolvaptan. Close monitoring, adequate water intake, and a clear sick-day plan are essential components of safe tolvaptan therapy [P1][P4].

These studies eventually motivated development of the V2 receptor antagonist, tolvaptan, which received FDA approval in 2018 [169]. In the first seminal clinical trial, the Tolvaptan Efficacy and Safety in Management of Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease and Its Outcomes (TEMPO) 3:4, tolvaptan, as compared with placebo, slowed the increase in total kidney volume and the decline in kidney function. Despite these outcomes, this first generation, small molecule drug also has undesirable drawbacks. Patients treated with tolvaptan exhibit adverse events related to aquaresis (excretion of dilute urine) and liver toxicity, which led to a higher discontinuation rate (23% dropout rate vs. 14% in the placebo group). Deranged liver function tests detected in clinical trials were found to be potentially life-threatening, and additional, major side effects include dry mouth, polyuria, hypernatremia, nausea/vomiting, and abdominal pain/cramps [170]. Notably, expected therapeutic outcomes have also been lackluster, as perfect adherence to tolvaptan treatment for 18 years is predicted to delay the time to ESRD by only 4.9 years [15]. The oral bioavailability of tolvaptan is expected to be 42% [171], and these drawbacks of off-target side effects and limited therapeutic efficacy calls for opportunities in precision medicine using

question: What new drugs are being developed for ADPKD? Are there any promising treatments in clinical trials right now?
gold_answer:
Several new drugs and repurposed agents are being developed for ADPKD, and a number are currently in clinical trials:

**Currently approved treatment:**
Tolvaptan (Jynarque®), a selective vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist, remains the only drug proven to attenuate ADPKD progression, having been approved by the FDA in 2018 [P1]. However, its long-term use causes serious side effects, particularly aquaresis-related adverse events, which limit its widespread use [P1][P5].

**Drugs in clinical trials or under investigation:**

1. **Somatostatin analogues:** Long-acting somatostatin analogues are being increasingly used to treat severe polycystic liver disease associated with ADPKD, though data on their renoprotective effects differ between studies and medications [P5][P6].

2. **Metformin:** Multiple clinical trials are underway evaluating metformin in adults with ADPKD (NCT02903511, NCT02656017, NCT03764605) [P3]. Metformin is a drug already marketed for other indications being investigated for off-label use in ADPKD [P5].

3. **Statins (Pravastatin):** In a randomized placebo-controlled trial in children and young adults (ages 8–22), pravastatin decreased the rate of total kidney volume (TKV) growth [P3]. However, benefit in older adults remains unclear, and a randomized trial in adults is underway (NCT03273413) [P3].

4. **Curcumin:** Clinical trials are underway evaluating curcumin in children and young adults with ADPKD aged 6–25 years (NCT02494141) [P3].

5. **Bardoxolone:** A clinical trial is evaluating bardoxolone in adults with ADPKD (NCT03918447), targeting oxidative stress pathways [P3].

6. **Tesevatinib:** This multikinase EGF/Src inhibitor is undergoing clinical trials in adults with ADPKD (NCT03203642), targeting pro-proliferative pathways involved in cyst enlargement [P3].

7. **CFTR (Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator) modulators:** These represent a novel class of drugs being investigated in clinical trials for ADPKD [P5].

8. **MicroRNA inhibitors:** These are also being investigated as a new therapeutic approach in clinical trials for ADPKD [P5].

**Drugs that showed limited success:**
mTOR inhibitors such as sirolimus appeared promising in mouse models but did not decrease kidney disease progression in clinical trials in adults and were associated with unacceptable side effects [P3].

**Key challenges:**
Because ADPKD is a chronic disease requiring lifelong treatment, and targets involved in cyst growth also have important functions in various physiological processes, there is a need to develop targeted therapies to minimize systemic side effects [P2]. Furthermore, discrepant results between preclinical and clinical studies remain a significant challenge [P2]. The high degree of interindividual heterogeneity in ADPKD also complicates drug development [P5].

Gansevoort1 · Esther Meijer1 Accepted: 22 June 2022 / Published online: 19 July 2022 © The Author(s) 2022 Abstract Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is characterized by progressive cyst formation that ultimately leads to kidney failure in most patients. Approximately 10% of patients who receive kidney replacement therapy suffer from ADPKD. To date, a vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist (V2RA) is the only drug that has been proven to attenuate disease progression. However, aquaresis-related adverse events limit its widespread use. Data on the renoprotective effects of somatostatin analogues differ largely between studies and medications. This review discusses new drugs that are investigated in clinical trials to treat ADPKD, such as cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulators and micro RNA inhibitors, and drugs already marketed for other indications that are being investigated for off-label use in ADPKD, such as metformin. In addition, potential methods to improve the tolerability of V2RAs are discussed, as well as methods to select patients with (likely) rapid disease progression and issues regarding the translation of preclinical data into clinical practice. question: Are there any new drugs being developed for PKD besides tolvaptan? I've heard that's the only approved one but I'm hoping there's more in the pipeline.
gold_answer:
You're correct that tolvaptan (Jynarque®) is currently the only FDA-approved drug specifically for ADPKD [P1][P3]. However, there is indeed a substantial pipeline of investigational therapies and approaches being explored. Here's a summary of what's in development:

**Drugs being repurposed or tested in clinical trials:**
Several FDA-approved drugs originally developed for other conditions have been investigated for PKD, including pioglitazone, sirolimus (rapamycin), and metformin, though results have been largely disappointing due to poor tolerability, off-target side effects, and/or limited efficacy in patients [P2]. Somatostatin analogues such as octreotide, which inhibit cAMP generation, are also being explored [P4]. Bosutinib (a Src/Bcr-Abl tyrosine kinase inhibitor) has shown some consistency between preclinical and clinical results in improving at least one ADPKD endpoint [P5]. Multiple other compounds are currently in clinical trials with results not yet available [P5].

**Preclinical approaches showing promise:**
Small molecules targeting additional perturbed signaling pathways have shown substantial promise in preclinical (animal model) studies. These include efforts to inhibit the mTOR pathway, aberrant ceramide production, the cell cycle, Wnt signaling, protein kinase activities, and epigenetic perturbations [P6]. Some ceramide-directed interventions have been evaluated in human clinical trials, but none have yet been shown to be safe and effective in slowing disease progression [P6]. More effective mTOR inhibitors such as rapamycin conjugates are also under investigation [P4]. Therapies addressing metabolic abnormalities (e.g., through food restriction) and reducing inflammation or calcium signaling are also being studied and may be approved for human treatment in the coming years [P4]. Combination treatments are another prospect being explored [P4].

**Computational drug repurposing:**
Researchers are using computational approaches such as "signature reversion" to identify FDA-approved drugs whose gene expression signatures inversely correlate with PKD disease signatures, potentially accelerating the identification of new therapeutic candidates while reducing cost and time compared to traditional drug discovery [P3].

**Emerging gene-based and novel therapies:**
Looking further ahead, several cutting-edge approaches are being investigated, including anti-microRNA (anti-miR) therapies, CRISPR-based gene editing technologies, and other gene therapy solutions that could potentially reverse cyst expansion rather than merely slow it [P2]. Other gene-targeted strategies include chaperone treatments for missense mutations, translational readthrough-inducing drugs for nonsense mutations, and approaches to boost levels of functional polycystin-1 (PC1) [P4]. Additionally, researchers are developing peptide-based strategies that could provide more targeted drug delivery to the kidneys, addressing the major bottleneck of nonspecific delivery that limits many small molecule therapeutics [P2].

So while tolvaptan remains the only approved option, there are indeed many approaches in the pipeline at various stages of development. It's important to note, however, that many compounds that looked promising in animal models have not yet demonstrated the same level of success in human trials [P5][P6].

Despite initial enthusiasm, these drugs have been largely disappointing due to poor tolerability, offtarget organ side effects, and/or limited therapeutic efficacy in patients [13]. Similarly, tolvaptan, the only FDA approved drug for autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), is difficult to tolerate due to side effects such as nausea and polyuria, and has been found to cause hepatotoxicity in certain patients [14]. Although tolvaptan slows cyst growth and delays the onset of renal failure by an average of ~5 years after 18 years of treatment, like other small molecules investigated for PKD, tolvaptan does not cure the underlying genetic defect of PKD [15]. Lasseigne1* Abstract Background Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is one of the most prevalent monogenic human diseases. It is mostly caused by pathogenic variants in PKD1 or PKD2 genes that encode interacting transmembrane proteins polycystin-1 (PC1) and polycystin-2 (PC2). Among many pathogenic processes described in ADPKD, those associated with cAMP signaling, inflammation, and metabolic reprogramming appear to regulate the disease manifestations. Tolvaptan, a vasopressin receptor-2 antagonist that regulates cAMP pathway, is the only FDAapproved ADPKD therapeutic. Tolvaptan reduces renal cyst growth and kidney function loss, but it is not tolerated by many patients and is associated with idiosyncratic liver toxicity. Therefore, additional therapeutic options for ADPKD treatment are needed. question: I'm on tolvaptan for my PKD. Does it work differently depending on how far along my kidney disease already is, or does it help the same at every stage?
gold_answer:
Great question — and the short answer is that tolvaptan does appear to help across a range of kidney function levels, but the degree and nature of its benefit may differ depending on how advanced your kidney disease is.

**Overall efficacy across stages:**
In the landmark TEMPO 3:4 trial, tolvaptan slowed both the increase in total kidney volume and the decline in kidney function compared to placebo [P1]. The subsequent REPRISE trial specifically enrolled patients with more advanced disease (eGFR 25–65 mL/min) and confirmed a slower decline in eGFR in the tolvaptan group (2.34 mL/min/yr vs. 3.61 mL/min/yr, p < 0.001) [P2]. So tolvaptan has demonstrated benefit in both earlier and later stages of ADPKD.

**Differences by stage:**
However, the benefit is not necessarily identical at every stage. A systematic review cited in the KDIGO 2025 guideline found that tolvaptan slowed eGFR decline by about 1.3 mL/min/1.73 m² per year (a 33% relative reduction), but this benefit was greatest in people aged ≤55 years who had an eGFR ≥25 mL/min [P3]. For those aged >55 years with lower eGFR (<45 mL/min), the evidence was less robust and shared decision-making is recommended [P3].

Interestingly, one small study examining short-term pharmacodynamic effects of tolvaptan at various levels of kidney function found that patients with lower baseline GFR (<60 mL/min) actually demonstrated more pronounced short-term changes in urine volume, free-water clearance, and kidney function markers compared to those with higher baseline GFR (>60 mL/min) [P6]. However, this was a small study without a control group, so results should be interpreted with caution [P6].

**Important practical considerations:**
Regardless of stage, tolvaptan treatment needs to be sustained for many years to prevent or delay kidney failure, and real-world adherence declines over time, with about 75% of people continuing treatment after 1–3 years [P5]. Even with perfect adherence over 18 years, tolvaptan is predicted to delay the time to end-stage renal disease by only about 4.9 years [P1]. Side effects — including excessive urination (polyuria), thirst, dehydration, and the risk of serious liver injury — must be carefully monitored at every stage [P1][P5].

**Bottom line:**
Tolvaptan helps across a range of kidney function, but the strongest evidence for benefit is in patients who are ≤55 years old with eGFR ≥25 mL/min and rapidly progressive disease [P3][P5]. If you have more advanced disease or are older, the decision to continue or start tolvaptan should involve a careful discussion with your nephrologist about the expected benefits versus side effects in your individual case [P3][P5].

These studies eventually motivated development of the V2 receptor antagonist, tolvaptan, which received FDA approval in 2018 [169]. In the first seminal clinical trial, the Tolvaptan Efficacy and Safety in Management of Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease and Its Outcomes (TEMPO) 3:4, tolvaptan, as compared with placebo, slowed the increase in total kidney volume and the decline in kidney function. Despite these outcomes, this first generation, small molecule drug also has undesirable drawbacks. Patients treated with tolvaptan exhibit adverse events related to aquaresis (excretion of dilute urine) and liver toxicity, which led to a higher discontinuation rate (23% dropout rate vs. 14% in the placebo group). Deranged liver function tests detected in clinical trials were found to be potentially life-threatening, and additional, major side effects include dry mouth, polyuria, hypernatremia, nausea/vomiting, and abdominal pain/cramps [170]. Notably, expected therapeutic outcomes have also been lackluster, as perfect adherence to tolvaptan treatment for 18 years is predicted to delay the time to ESRD by only 4.9 years [15]. The oral bioavailability of tolvaptan is expected to be 42% [171], and these drawbacks of off-target side effects and limited therapeutic efficacy calls for opportunities in precision medicine using

question: What's the safest pain medication for someone with PKD? I get back pain from my enlarged kidneys and I'm nervous about taking anything that could hurt them further.
gold_answer:
I understand your concern about protecting your kidneys while managing pain — this is a very common and important issue for people with PKD. Here is what the current guidelines recommend:

**Start with non-drug approaches first.** Nonpharmacologic, noninvasive interventions should generally be considered as the initial treatment of chronic kidney pain in ADPKD [P1][P3]. These include physical therapy interventions such as heat pads, ice massage, light exercise, and/or whirlpool treatments [P1]. Improving body posture and mechanics (for example, the Alexander technique) may help, especially since enlarged kidneys can cause abnormal posture that worsens back pain [P1][P2]. A support garment may also help if your pain is due to renal pedicle traction from enlarged kidneys [P1]. Cognitive–behavioral therapy, acupuncture, and transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) are additional options, though acupuncture and TENS are generally reserved for pain not responsive to nonopioid medications [P2].

**When medications are needed, acetaminophen (Tylenol) is the safest first-line drug for chronic pain control in PKD** [P2]. This is important because it does not carry the kidney risks that other pain relievers do.

**NSAIDs (such as ibuprofen, naproxen) should be avoided for chronic pain.** The guidelines specifically discourage NSAID use for chronic pain management in ADPKD. However, NSAIDs may be used short-term for acute pain episodes in people who have stable kidney function [P2]. This is a key distinction — occasional short-term use may be acceptable, but regular or chronic use is not recommended because of the potential for kidney harm.

**If acetaminophen alone is not enough**, tricyclic antidepressants and gabapentin may be useful as analgesic adjuvants, though there are no randomized controlled trials specifically in ADPKD [P2]. Experience with pregabalin in ADPKD is still limited [P2]. Tramadol can be used as a next-line agent or as adjunctive therapy when the previous drugs do not adequately control pain [P2][P5]. Clonidine is another option when acetaminophen and tramadol are not effective or are contraindicated [P5].

**Opioids should be reserved as a last resort**, used only after failure or inadequacy of all previous approaches [P5]. If needed, they should be limited and supervised [P5].

**Overall, managing chronic pain in PKD requires a stepwise, multidisciplinary approach** [P4][P3], and shared decision-making between you and your healthcare provider should guide your pain management strategy [P3]. If your pain becomes refractory or complex, referral to a multidisciplinary team including nephrology, pain specialists (algology), physiotherapy, psychology, and other specialties is recommended [P3][P5]. You should also be screened for depression if pain is refractory [P5].

In summary: **acetaminophen is the safest medication**, NSAIDs should generally be avoided for ongoing use, and non-drug strategies should be tried first or used alongside medications.

supporting_passages.text: in patients with more advanced ADPKD (18–64 years of age, eGFR 25–60 ml/min/1.73 m2) because it was considered to be detrimental in these patients207. At both early and late stages of the disease, monotherapy with an angiotensin-converting-enzyme (ACE) inhibitor achieved good blood pressure control (<130/85 mmHg) in most patients. Therefore, ACE inhibitors (or angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs)) are considered to be the first-line therapy for hypertension in ADPKD. Pain.—At least 60% of patients with ADPKD eventually experience acute or chronic abdominal or flank pain of renal origin. Acute pain is often associated with kidney cyst haemorrhage, infection or nephrolithiasis (kidney stones). Chronic pain is usually attributable to cyst enlargement that causes kidney capsule stretching or to marked enlargement of the kidneys and/or liver that causes musculoskeletal back pain. Managing chronic pain can be challenging and requires a multidisciplinary stepwise approach with multiple strategies, including physical therapy interventions, pain medications, cyst sclerosis and laparoscopic cyst fenestrations and renal denervation procedures208–210. Haematuria.—Gross haematuria may be associated with cyst haemorrhage or infection, passage of kidney stones or kidney or urinary tract neoplasms. Cyst haemorrhage can be associated with fever, which might be difficult to differentiate from cyst infection. With conservative management (rest, holding anticoagulants or antiplatelet drugs, and analgesics if needed), most haemorrhages resolve within 2–7 days. If symptoms persist for >1 week

question: I was just diagnosed with ADPKD. What kind of medication options are currently available to slow down cyst growth?
gold_answer:
As a newly diagnosed ADPKD patient, here is an overview of the medication options currently available or being investigated to slow cyst growth:

**FDA-Approved Treatment:**

The only FDA-approved medication specifically for ADPKD is **tolvaptan**, a vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist. It works by blocking the V2 receptor, which reduces cyclic AMP (cAMP) production — a key driver of cyst growth and fluid secretion [P6][P1]. Tolvaptan has been shown to slow total kidney volume (TKV) growth and GFR decline, and it may delay the onset of renal failure by an average of approximately 5 years after 18 years of treatment [P3]. However, it is currently recommended only for adults at risk for rapid ADPKD progression, as it can cause side effects including significant aquaresis (excessive water loss), nausea, polyuria, and potentially hepatotoxicity (liver injury) [P3][P6].

**Medications Under Investigation:**

Several other pharmacologic agents are being studied in clinical trials:

1. **Metformin** – Originally used for type 2 diabetes, metformin activates AMPK, which inhibits several cystogenic pathways. It may also slow cyst progression by reducing glucose availability to cyst epithelial cells [P2]. Clinical trials such as the TAME trial are currently recruiting ADPKD patients to evaluate metformin's safety and effects on kidney volume, renal function, pain, and quality of life [P2][P4].

2. **Statins (e.g., pravastatin)** – In a randomized trial in children and young adults with ADPKD (ages 8–22), pravastatin was found to decrease the rate of TKV growth [P4]. However, a post-hoc analysis of adult participants in the HALT PKD study found no benefit, and a dedicated randomized trial in adults is underway [P4].

3. **Somatostatin analogues** – These act on somatostatin receptors (SSTR) to reduce cAMP-driven cyst growth [P1].

4. **mTOR inhibitors (e.g., sirolimus)** – Although these showed promise in mouse models, clinical trials in adults with ADPKD did not show a decrease in kidney disease progression and were associated with unacceptable side effects [P4].

5. **Bardoxolone** – Currently undergoing clinical trials in adults with ADPKD [P4].

6. **Curcumin** – Being evaluated in clinical trials for children and young adults with ADPKD (ages 6–25) [P4].

7. **Tesevatinib** – A multikinase EGF/Src inhibitor currently in clinical trials for adults with ADPKD [P4].

8. **Pioglitazone** – Has been explored as a repurposed drug but has been largely disappointing due to poor tolerability and limited therapeutic efficacy [P3].

**Important Considerations:**

It is important to note that none of these medications cure the underlying genetic defect of ADPKD, and no current therapies can reverse cyst expansion [P3]. Emerging approaches such as anti-microRNA therapies, CRISPR-based gene editing, and other gene therapy solutions may hold future promise but are still in early stages of investigation [P3]. Additionally, non-pharmacological approaches such as dietary interventions that induce ketosis are being explored as potential complementary strategies to slow cyst growth [P5].

You should discuss with your nephrologist whether tolvaptan or enrollment in a clinical trial for one of the investigational agents is appropriate for your specific situation, based on your risk of rapid progression and overall health profile.

[Figure from page 9] The figure illustrates the biochemical mechanisms underlying autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) and highlights various therapeutic targets being explored. Below is a detailed description of the figure: ### Overview - The figure is a schematic detailing the pathways and interactions involved in the pathophysiology of ADPKD, with a focus on treatment mechanisms. ### Components 1. **Cyst Fluid and Membrane Interactions**: - Depicted at the center, cyst fluid interacts with the apical membrane of renal epithelial cells. - Various receptors and pathways are illustrated surrounding the cyst fluid. 2. **Receptors and Pathways**: - **V2R (Vasopressin receptor)**: Activated by vasopressin, promotes cAMP production which is crucial in cyst growth. - **SSTR (Somatostatin receptor)**: Related to the action of somatostatin analogues and the reduction of cyst growth. - **Foliate receptors**: Involved in cellular uptake of folate and potentially correlated with cyst development. - **EGFR (Epidermal growth factor receptor)**: Inhibitors targeting this receptor are considered significant for therapeutic intervention. - **mTOR (mammalian target of rapamycin)**: Major pathway regulating cell growth and proliferation, influenced by various stimuli.

Recent efforts have focused on the use of drugs already approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for other diseases that have also shown efficacy in delaying cystogenesis, including pioglitazone, sirolimus, and metformin (Table 1) [9–12]. Despite initial enthusiasm, these drugs have been largely disappointing due to poor tolerability, offtarget organ side effects, and/or limited therapeutic efficacy in patients [13]. Similarly, tolvaptan, the only FDA approved drug for autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), is difficult to tolerate due to side effects such as nausea and polyuria, and has been found to cause hepatotoxicity in certain patients [14]. Although tolvaptan slows cyst growth and delays the onset of renal failure by an average of ~5 years after 18 years of treatment, like other small molecules investigated for PKD, tolvaptan does not cure the underlying genetic defect of PKD [15]. Currently, no clinical trials exist for therapies that reverse cyst expansion, but emerging anti-micro RNA (anti-miR) [16, 17], clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) technologies [18], or other gene therapy solutions may provide promise for PKD reversal upon further investigation. A major bottleneck in the clinical translation of these small molecule therapeutics however is their nonspecific and untargeted

Several pharmacologic agents targeting these pathways are being studied in ADPKD, including statins, curcumin, metformin, and bardoxolone [43, 84-89]. In a randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial in children and young adults with ADPKD aged 8-22 years, pravastatin was found to decrease the rate of TKV growth [90]. It is unclear whether statins provide benefit in older adults with ADPKD. A post-hoc analysis of adult participants in the HALT PKD study found no benefit of statins on ADPKD progression [91]; a randomized trial in adults is now underway (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03273413) that will help to address this question more definitively. Clinical trials are also underway to evaluate curcumin in children and young adults with ADPKD aged 6-25 years (NCT02494141), metformin in adults with ADPKD (NCT02903511, NCT02656017, NCT03764605), and bardoxolone in adults with ADPKD (NCT03918447). Pro-proliferative pathways that are involved in cyst enlargement in ADPKD include the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) [92], and epidermal growth factor (EGF)/Src pathways. mTOR inhibitors such as sirolimus appeared promising to reduce ADPKD progression in mouse models [93]. However, in clinical trials in adults with ADPKD, sirolimus did not decrease kidney disease progression and was associated with unacceptable side effects [94, 95]. The multikinase EGF/Src inhibitor tesevatinib is currently undergoing clinical trials in adults with ADPKD (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT03203642). As mentioned previously,

In contrast to kidney involvement, genotype does not appear to be a significant predictor of the severity of polycystic liver disease in patients with ADPKD [73]. Pharmacologic agents to prevent ADPKD progression Pathophysiologic mechanisms of cyst enlargement in ADPKD involve a number of biological pathways. The cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) pathway is upregulated in PKD cystic epithelia [74], leading to increased cell proliferation and fluid secretion [10]. cAMP activity can be downregulated by antagonism of arginine vasopressin (AVP) activity. The vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist tolvaptan has been shown to slow TKV growth and GFR decline, and is now FDA-approved for patients with ADPKD over 18 years of age [57]. Given the potential for side effects such as aquaresis and liver dysfunction [57], and the burden of potentially taking a medication for decades, tolvaptan is only recommended for adults at risk for rapid ADPKD progression [66]. question: I've heard about tolvaptan as a treatment for PKD. How does it work, and does it work the same for people at different stages of kidney function?
gold_answer:
Tolvaptan (brand name Jynarque®) is a selective antagonist of the vasopressin V2 receptor (V2R) [P3]. In ADPKD, vasopressin binds to the V2 receptor on renal epithelial cells, which promotes the production of cyclic AMP (cAMP) — a key driver of cyst growth and fluid secretion [P2]. By blocking this receptor, tolvaptan reduces renal cAMP levels, thereby slowing both cyst expansion and the decline in kidney function [P1]. In the landmark TEMPO 3:4 clinical trial, tolvaptan, compared with placebo, slowed the increase in total kidney volume and the decline in kidney function [P1].

However, tolvaptan does not work equally well at all stages of kidney disease. A systematic review of randomized controlled trials showed that tolvaptan slowed eGFR decline by approximately 1.3 ml/min per 1.73 m² per year (a 33% relative reduction compared with placebo), but this benefit was greatest in people aged 55 years or younger who had an eGFR of at least 25 ml/min per 1.73 m² [P4]. For people older than 55 years, particularly those with an eGFR below 45 ml/min per 1.73 m², the evidence is less robust, and shared, individualized decision-making is recommended when considering whether to start tolvaptan in this group [P4]. The treatment needs to be sustained for many years to prevent or delay kidney failure, and real-world adherence declines over time, with about 75% of people continuing treatment after 1–3 years [P6]. Even with perfect adherence over 18 years, tolvaptan is predicted to delay the time to end-stage renal disease (ESRD) by only about 4.9 years [P1].

It is also important to be aware of significant side effects. Tolvaptan carries a black-box warning for potential hepatotoxicity and fatal liver failure [P5]. Other common adverse effects include aquaresis-related symptoms such as polyuria, thirst, dehydration, dry mouth, hypernatremia, nausea/vomiting, and abdominal pain [P1][P6]. These side effects led to a higher discontinuation rate in clinical trials (23% in the tolvaptan group versus 14% in the placebo group) [P1]. Patient selection for treatment should be based on criteria indicating rapidly progressive kidney disease, the absence of contraindications, and the patient's ability to tolerate the treatment and maintain adequate hydration [P6]. To date, no trials have examined the efficacy of tolvaptan in cystic kidney diseases other than ADPKD (such as ARPKD or tuberous sclerosis–associated cystic kidney disease), and its use is not advised in these conditions [P4].

Finally, it is worth noting that because other cAMP agonists (such as forskolin and lysophosphatidic acid) are also elevated in ADPKD cyst fluid and can stimulate V2 receptor–independent fluid secretion, the efficacy of tolvaptan may be inherently limited by these alternative pathways [P5].

supporting_passages.text: have found that treatment with V2 receptor antagonists can reduce cystic burden in orthologous animal models of human PKD by reducing renal cAMP levels [87, 168]. These studies eventually motivated development of the V2 receptor antagonist, tolvaptan, which received FDA approval in 2018 [169]. In the first seminal clinical trial, the Tolvaptan Efficacy and Safety in Management of Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease and Its Outcomes (TEMPO) 3:4, tolvaptan, as compared with placebo, slowed the increase in total kidney volume and the decline in kidney function. Despite these outcomes, this first generation, small molecule drug also has undesirable drawbacks. Patients treated with tolvaptan exhibit adverse events related to aquaresis (excretion of dilute urine) and liver toxicity, which led to a higher discontinuation rate (23% dropout rate vs. 14% in the placebo group). Deranged liver function tests detected in clinical trials were found to be potentially life-threatening, and additional, major side effects include dry mouth, polyuria, hypernatremia, nausea/vomiting, and abdominal pain/cramps [170]. Notably, expected therapeutic outcomes have also been lackluster, as perfect adherence to tolvaptan treatment for 18 years is predicted to delay the time to ESRD by only 4.9 years [15]. question: Are there any new drugs being developed for ADPKD besides tolvaptan? What kinds of treatments are in the pipeline?
gold_answer:
Yes, beyond tolvaptan — which remains the only FDA-approved disease-modifying treatment for ADPKD [P4] — there are several promising therapeutic approaches in various stages of development:

**1. Repurposed existing drugs:**
- **Cinacalcet**, an FDA-approved calcium-sensing receptor (CaSR) activator, is being investigated for repurposing in ADPKD. It works by reducing cAMP levels, which can reduce cell proliferation, cystogenesis, and cyst enlargement. A major advantage is that, as an already-approved drug with a favorable safety profile, it could potentially be tested in clinical ADPKD trials without the need for extensive long-term safety studies [P2]. Notably, the CaSR activator R-568 has shown synergistic effects when used in combination with tolvaptan in mouse and rat models of ADPKD [P2].

**2. Inhibitors of cAMP generation:**
- **Somatostatin analogues** (such as octreotide) are being studied as inhibitors of cAMP generation, a key driver of cyst formation [P3].

**3. mTOR pathway inhibitors:**
- More effective inhibitors of mTOR activity specifically targeted to the kidneys, such as **rapamycin conjugates**, are under development [P3].

**4. CFTR inhibitors:**
- CFTR (cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator) inhibitors have been proposed to reduce cyst enlargement, given the well-known role of CFTR and cAMP-induced chloride secretion in cyst growth. However, a limitation is that they reduce cyst growth without affecting cell proliferation or cystogenesis, and they face a significant barrier in the need for extensive long-term safety studies [P2].

**5. Metabolic interventions:**
- Approaches targeting the metabolic abnormalities in ADPKD cells are being explored. These include dietary interventions such as calorie restriction and ketosis-inducing diets, which have slowed disease progression in animal models. **2-deoxyglucose**, which starves glycolysis-dependent cyst cells, shows great promise in preclinical studies [P5]. Food restriction to remedy metabolic abnormalities is also being explored [P3].

**6. Anti-inflammatory and calcium signaling therapies:**
- Therapies aimed at reducing inflammation or targeting calcium signaling have shown effectiveness in rodent studies and are likely candidates for future human treatment [P3].

**7. Combination treatments:**
- There are prospects for combination therapies that target multiple pathways simultaneously [P3], such as the synergistic combination of CaSR activators with tolvaptan [P2].

**8. Gene-targeted and precision approaches:**
- More direct approaches targeting the basic defect in PKD (deficiency of polycystin-1 or polycystin-2) are being considered. These include **chaperone treatment** for some missense mutations, **translational readthrough-inducing drugs** for nonsense mutations, strategies to boost levels of functional PC1, and **somatic gene editing using CRISPR-Cas9** to repair the underlying genetic defect in the kidneys [P3].

Despite this rich pipeline, it is important to note that a major challenge for all new ADPKD drugs is the need for extensive long-term safety studies, given that ADPKD is a chronic, slowly progressing condition [P2][P6]. Additionally, results from preclinical animal models have not always translated well to clinical studies, and experts recommend testing in at least two different rodent models before proceeding to clinical trials [P6]. There remains a substantial unmet need for additional effective treatments [P4][P1].

The successful development of Tolvaptan was clearly predicated upon the preclinical studies that identified aberrant cAMP signaling as a driver of ADPKD cyst formation. Equally importantly, however, the effort that led to its successful development owes at least as much to the enormous effort invested by a large number of clinical and translational scientists in developing the metrics that were required to assess therapeutic efficacy and who designed and conducted the trials that employed these metrics. The approval of Tolvaptan offers the ADPKD patient community with a long-awaited therapeutic option that can slow disease progression and prolong renal function. Its value to the ADPKD community also derives from the fact that the remarkable community of investigators who moved Tolvaptan from the laboratory to the clinic pioneered a pathway for the testing and approval of future therapies that may offer better efficacy and side effect profiles. Metabolism The metabolic alterations that characterize cells that lack polycystin expression suggest attractive therapeutic vulnerabilities. Dietary interventions, including calorie restriction and imposition of ketosis-inducing diets, have been shown to slow disease progression in animal models of ADPKD (293, 374, 436). Starving glycolysis-dependent cyst cells for glycolytic substrates by administering 2-deoxyglucose shows great potential promise (373, 419, 437).
